translation: [All] praise is [due] to Allah, who has sent down upon His Servant the Book and has not made therein any deviance
commentary:
ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَلَمْ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عِوَجَا ۜ

*All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah, who sent down the Book upon His slave, and He has not placed any crookedness in it.*

By Allah's grace, we begin our study of Surah Al-Kahf, one of the most frequently recited surahs in Islamic tradition. This practice is rooted in the guidance of the Prophet ﷺ, who encouraged us to recite it on Fridays. Among the many virtues mentioned about this surah, scholars have reached near unanimous consensus that it offers protection from the trial of the Dajjal, the greatest tribulation that will befall this Ummah and all of humanity.

Minister Hasan Dehlawi, a distinguished Indian scholar, offered a profound observation about this surah. He argued that the Prophet ﷺ didn't merely instruct us to recite Al-Kahf for protection from the Dajjal through simple recitation alone. Rather, the teachings and lessons contained within this surah directly address how to deal with that great trial. The surah itself responds to the immense tribulations that will fall upon the world.

### A Timeline of Divine Guidance

There's a remarkable thematic connection between this surah and the one before it. Surah Al-Isra opened by discussing Jewish history, the story of the previous Muslims. Surah Al-Kahf, which immediately follows, is directly tied to the future of the current Muslims. It's as if we have a continuous timeline: the history of the former community and the future of the present one, seamlessly woven together.

From a literary perspective, notice how the end of the previous surah commands us: "Say Alhamdulillah." When someone tells you to say something, what do you do? You say it. This surah responds to that call by opening with Alhamdulillah. The surahs are beautifully linked together in this way.

### The Honor of Servitude

The status of a messenger, particularly our Messenger ﷺ, reached its pinnacle when he was honored with the miraculous night journey. He traveled not only to meet with other prophets, but eventually ascended to Allah ﷻ Himself. Though we often think of this as two events, Al-Isra and Al-Mi'raj, they are really one continuous journey with a stop in the middle at Al-Aqsa.

Before this point in history, the highest honor a human being had ever reached was atop a mountain. Musa (AS) had spoken to Allah ﷻ, and that was the greatest status any human being had physically attained. But even that tremendous honor was surpassed by what the Prophet ﷺ achieved, what is also called Al-Maqam Al-Mahmud in the previous surah. The Messenger ﷺ ascended to the seventh heaven to receive revelation directly.

Yet no matter how much a slave rises, he remains a slave. This is crucial to understand. In human language, there is no word to describe a person lower in status than a slave. No one is beneath a slave in worldly hierarchies. But in Islam, when you become a slave of Allah ﷻ, there is no higher status than that.

What's remarkable about the Mi'raj is that revelation didn't travel to the Prophet ﷺ as it usually does. Instead, the slave traveled up to receive revelation. Typically, the angel comes to you, the book comes to you, the message comes to you. But in Al-Isra, Allah ﷻ honored the slave by having him ascend to revelation. The words used were سُبْحَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ - Glory be to the One who took His slave on a journey by night. This emphasized that the Messenger traveled for revelation.

Now, in this surah, we return to the normal pattern. الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ - All praise belongs to Allah who sent the Book down upon His slave. In one case, the slave travels; in the other, the revelation travels. In both instances, guidance is being given to the Messenger, but two different aspects are being highlighted.

Here's what's beautiful: in either case, Allah ﷻ didn't say "His Messenger" or use the Prophet's name ﷺ. He highlighted the term we associate with the lowest worldly status and taught us that this term actually represents the highest status you can reach. You would think that in these two exclusive cases - the night journey and receiving revelation - the word used for the Prophet ﷺ would be something unique that nobody else shares with him. But the word used is عَبْد - slave. We share this word with the Messenger ﷺ all the time.

There's a powerful lesson here. We do not separate ourselves from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ in this: that he is a slave, and that is the highest honor a human being can attain. This is also important because the followers of Musa (AS) did not understand what it means to honor a messenger properly. On one end of the spectrum, Christians elevated their messenger so high that they no longer think of him as a slave at all. In this surah, the proper balance is struck: the Book is brought down to the Messenger, while he remains identified as Allah's slave.

### The Third Alhamdulillah

This is the third surah in the Quran that begins with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ. Al-Fatiha was the first, Al-An'am was the second, and now Al-Kahf is the third. In each surah that begins with praise to Allah ﷻ, there's a different lesson taught, a different reason to be grateful.

In this surah, Allah ﷻ says something significant. One scholar explained that the point here is to clarify for us that Allah ﷻ doesn't just master creation in a physical way only. There's another kind of ownership and mastery - a spiritual ownership. Allah ﷻ owns the tree and ensures its physical growth. He owns the human being and ensures his physical growth. But He also ensures spiritual growth.

This ayah mentions the true status and purpose of human creation. The human being wasn't created merely for his physical, mortal existence, but for a higher, more noble purpose: to know and recognize his Maintainer and Master, and to learn the religion.

### No Crookedness in the Book

الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - All praise belongs to Allah who sent down the Book upon His slave.

Then Allah ﷻ says: وَلَمْ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عِوَجًا (wa lam yaj'al lahu 'iwaja) - and He did not place any crookedness in it.

The word عِوَج in Arabic is used both literally and figuratively. Literally, it means deviation or crookedness. You can use it for a line that's bumpy, a road with bumps on it, or a cane that's curved. But 'iwaj can also refer to a bodily flaw, like when someone can't walk straight and has crookedness in their gait.

In the figurative sense, 'iwaj means something complicated, something that's not clear, something that seems to have confusion or flaws in it. When you talk straight, you know where the conversation is going. When a road is straight, you know where it leads. But when a road curves, you don't know where it's going. Similarly, when an argument is "curved," you don't know where the argument is headed. Someone talks to you for ten minutes and you think, "Where is this going? There's too much 'iwaj in your conversation."

Allah ﷻ says He did not put any crookedness in the Quran. This teaches us a particular approach to every surah: each one is leading somewhere. It's not randomly putting things together that go this way and that way. Each surah is heading you in a direction, and that direction is very straight.

translation: [He has made it] straight, to warn of severe punishment from Him and to give good tidings to the believers who do righteous deeds that they will have a good reward
commentary:
قَيِّمًا لِّيُنذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِّن لَّدُنْهُ وَيُبَشِّرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا

*Perfectly upright, to warn of intense punishment from Him, and to give good news to the believers who do righteous deeds that they will have an excellent reward.*

قَيِّمًا means something that remains upright. It also means something that maintains other things, that holds other things up, like a foundation holds a building. The foundation holding a building up is also a form of qayyim. This quality that Allah ﷻ gives to the Quran means two things: first, the Quran itself is not deviated. Second, it keeps society and the people who believe in it from deviating. The Book itself has no deviation, and it ensures that believers don't deviate. They remain standing straight, and this straightness is maintained across generations.

Why is this emphasized? Because previous revelations came, but eventually 'iwaj - deviation - was introduced into them. When that deviation was introduced, it wasn't just in the revelation itself; the people who followed those scriptures also deviated as a result. So the first requirement is that the Book should be free of deviation. The second is that the Book should not just be pure in its form, but should actively do its job of keeping the people in line.

### A Warning of Intense War

لِّيُنذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِّن لَّدُنْهُ (li-yundhira ba'san shadidan min ladunhu) - so that it may warn of intense war from Him.

The word بَأْس means difficulty, but it can also be used for economic trouble, social trouble, and in the worst case scenario, it means war itself. Actually, it encompasses everything surrounding war: the economic troubles, the social troubles that precede and accompany conflict. So the Quran comes to warn of an intense war.

That by itself is already terrible. What makes it worse? The word شَدِيد - intense, severe. And if that wasn't bad enough, there's مِن لَّدُنْهُ - from Him.

The words لَدُن and لَدَى both mean "to have" or "from," but there's a crucial difference. If I say عِندِي سَيَّارَة - I have a car - it means I own a car somewhere. But if I say لَدَيَّ سَيَّارَة, it means not only do I have it, but it's immediately accessible to me right now, within reach.

When you say عِنْد, you have something, but it may not be accessible to you. When you say لَدُن, you have something and it is immediately accessible, ready to be deployed. So مِن لَّدُنْهُ means this war is more feasible, more immediate, right there with instant access from Allah ﷻ.

The word لَدُن itself makes this war unavoidable because it's a divine decree. It's going to happen, and it's especially from Him. The way Allah ﷻ structures this language shows that this war cannot be avoided. If He has prepared this intense war Himself, and it's going to happen by His decree, then people need to be warned about it.

What war is Allah ﷻ talking about? Traditional scholars say this refers to the end of times, the trials of the Dajjal, and the return of 'Isa (AS). They speak of the enormous conflicts that will take place, the massacres of human beings, all the catastrophes and calamities that will unfold. The Quran came to warn about that.

### Who Receives the Warning, Who Gets the Good News

Now notice something remarkable. When you warn, you should mention who you're warning, right? There are two objects in a warning: who you're warning and what you're warning about.

How many objects are mentioned in the warning in this ayah? Just one: بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا - intense war. But who is being warned? It's left unmentioned. Everyone.

Then Allah ﷻ says: وَيُبَشِّرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - and to give good news to the believers. When it comes to congratulations, who is mentioned? The believers.

This is beautiful. Allah ﷻ didn't even honor the disbelievers by mentioning them in the warning, but He honored the believers by mentioning them specifically. The warning is open to all - Muslims and non-Muslims should equally fear this coming war. But the congratulations, the good news? That's specifically for the believers.

And even within the believers, when those times arrive, it won't be enough to simply call yourself a believer. You'll have to meet a qualification: ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - those who do righteous deeds. Usually, the word مُؤْمِن (believer) stands on its own without needing further qualification. But this situation, this trial, is going to be so immense that even the most explicit term - mu'min - had to be qualified further. Only those who act righteously will receive the good news.

أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا - that they will have an excellent reward. When the warning came, what did it warn of? بَأْس - war and hardship in this world. But when the good news came, what was it about? أَجْر - reward in the Hereafter.

This is profound. The believers are being told that those times will be so tough, don't expect reward in this world. Those times are going to be so difficult, Allah ﷻ isn't going to congratulate you with worldly success. He's giving you the promise of أَجْرًا حَسَنًا - excellent reward in the afterlife. You're going to work toward that.

So you might not see the consequences of your good deeds in this world. You might not see things getting any better. But that should not stop you from continuing your righteous actions, because your rewards aren't going to be here, especially during those times. They're going to come in the end, they're going to be in the Hereafter.

translation: In which they will remain forever
commentary:
مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا

*Remaining in it forever.*

مَّـٰكِثِينَ - such a beautiful word. What did Musa (AS) say to his family? اِمْكُثُوا - "Stay here." The word مُكْث means to remain somewhere in anticipation of something. When you remain somewhere waiting for something to happen, that's mukth. Musa's (AS) family was going to stay down in the darkness until he returned, so they were in a state of mukth - remaining in anticipation.

Now look at this ayah: مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا - remaining in it forever. The translation usually just says "they will remain in Jannah forever," and yes, that's accurate. But there's added flavor here, something special about Jannah that this word captures.

You'll be in Jannah for eternity. If you're young enough for this eternity, chances are you might wonder: could I get bored? Think about what brings excitement to life. For those following sports playoffs, what creates excitement? Anticipation. When there are ten seconds left, two timeouts remaining, down by two points - what's going to happen? There's anticipation of what comes next.

Excitement and joy are usually associated not just with what's happening right now, but with what is coming. Jannah is going to be eternal. Might someone get bored? Someone might imagine: I'll never miss a free throw in Jannah. Won't that get boring after a while? Where's the challenge?

But Allah ﷻ says: for eternity, you will be anticipating better things to come. For eternity, you'll be waiting for even more goodness, and even more after that, and even more after that. The excitement never dies.

Think about when someone first moves to a new house. They're so excited about everything. They give everyone the tour: "Check out our kitchen! It has cabinets! Let me show you our toilet - watch how the pump works, it's amazing!" You're excited about every detail. But after ten or fifteen years living in the same house, do you still give tours? No, you're thinking about moving.

But in Jannah? مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا - there's always anticipation of more. There's always something better around the corner. Forever.

translation: And to warn those who say, "Allah has taken a son
commentary:
**وَيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا**

*And to warn those who say, "Allah has taken a son."*

The Quran's straightness serves multiple purposes. Among them is to warn those who claim that Allah ﷻ has taken a son. Notice the deliberate structure here: earlier in the Surah, when Allah ﷻ is mentioned, the reference addresses the Jews. Now, at the beginning of this ayah, the focus shifts to the Christians. This isn't merely a theological correction—it's a warning about the profound consequences this belief would unleash upon the world.

Understanding this warning requires us to recognize why the Quran's preservation is so critical. Previous scriptures underwent deviation, but Allah ﷻ always sent a new messenger as a safeguard. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is the final messenger, which means there will be no more messengers after him. Therefore, this Book must remain intact and straight until the end of times. The missions that were once carried out by messengers—giving glad tidings and warning—must now be fulfilled by the Quran itself.

translation: They have no knowledge of it, nor had their fathers. Grave is the word that comes out of their mouths; they speak not except a lie
commentary:
**مَّا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَلَا لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ ۚ كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۚ إِن يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِبًا**

*They have no knowledge of this, nor did their forefathers. Grave is the word that comes out of their mouths; they speak not except a lie.*

Allah ﷻ doesn't simply reject their claim—He demolishes any foundation for it. The phrase مَّا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ (they have no knowledge of it) is emphatic in its negation. The particle مَا negates, and then مِنْ عِلْمٍ adds even more force: not any knowledge whatsoever. They may dress up their theology with elaborate allegories and complex explanations that sound sophisticated, but Allah ﷻ calls it out for exactly what it is—they have zero knowledge.

This lack of knowledge extends beyond just the current generation: وَلَا لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ—nor was this kind of knowledge in possession of their ancestry. Their forefathers didn't have it either. There's no chain of authentic transmission, no basis in revelation.

Then comes a powerful statement: كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ—how enormous, what a grave matter is the word that comes out of their mouths. Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say كَلَام (speech) here. He said كَلِمَة (a word)—a single word. The single word of shirk (associating partners with Allah) that comes out of their mouths is enormous in its consequences. The ayah concludes definitively: إِن يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِبًا—they say nothing but a lie.

translation: Then perhaps you would kill yourself through grief over them, [O Muhammad], if they do not believe in this message, [and] out of sorrow
commentary:
**فَلَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ إِن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَسَفًا**

*Then perhaps you would kill yourself through grief over them, [O Muhammad], if they do not believe in this message, [and] out of sorrow.*

Now Allah ﷻ addresses His Messenger ﷺ directly with remarkable tenderness. The word بَاخِعٌ comes from a root that means to cause someone to grieve or become so enraged with sadness that they effectively destroy themselves from within. It's a form of killing without a weapon—you agitate someone to death, you grieve someone to death. Some people literally die of grief after losing a loved one; they consume themselves from the inside.

Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ: perhaps you are going to kill yourself—not physically with a weapon, but through overwhelming grief. The phrase عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ is particularly meaningful. آثَار literally means footsteps, traces, remains, consequences—what is left in the wake of something. When you see destroyed homes, you know a storm passed through. When you see a giant hole in the ground, you know an explosion occurred. When you see a building burned down, you know there was a fire.

Allah ﷻ is saying: you might kill yourself out of grief when you see the consequences, the wake of destruction that Christian faith is going to leave behind. The Prophet ﷺ, in his wisdom, could see where this was leading. Let's explore what these consequences would be.

The Christian faith centers on accepting the Trinity, with one of its fundamental premises being that God has a son. For centuries, they've been attempting to make sense of this for their followers. When Christian Europe entered the Dark Ages, they turned to the Muslim world for education. They came to Al-Andalus and other Muslim lands to study. Many of their books had been burned, and whatever remained was preserved by Muslims who had translated them into Arabic. Christian students actually studied Greek philosophy in Arabic and then translated it back to their own languages.

The Muslim scholars were studying this material to examine and refute it, while Christians came to learn. There was a real risk for Christian Europe—these students might become Muslim and never return. Indeed, many of their brightest sons embraced Islam and became assets to Islamic civilization rather than returning home. However, a significant number held onto their faith, went back, and created what we call the Renaissance and the French Enlightenment.

These returning students brought back what they had learned from Muslims and began discussing science and reasoning in Europe. But when they started talking about science and rational inquiry, there was an obvious clash with Christian faith. Christianity held a position of power and tried to crush this conversation violently. They burned libraries, hung people publicly, and developed elaborate torture devices. They would tie people up and slowly stretch them apart until bones separated one at a time. While torturing people, they would tell their victims, "I'm doing you a favor—you're suffering in this world, so your heaven is guaranteed."

As a result of this oppression in the name of religion, the Western people developed a hatred not just for Christianity, but for religion itself. Secularism was born from this reaction. The secular argument was simple: religion hates science, reason, rational thought and intellectual freedom. Therefore, religion should be removed from public life. If you want to keep religion, fine—keep it personal and private. But it should never have power again, because religion is for the closed-minded.

When a Westerner tells you that religion is closed-minded, they're speaking from a very real historical experience. This reaction to Christianity extended to all religions. When you develop a hatred for religion, you stop caring about God altogether. The thinking became: God isn't going to build roads and highways—science will. God isn't going to build factories or create a world economy—science will. God isn't going to send angels as an army—we have to build cannons and guns ourselves, and science helps us do that.

The narrative shifted: "We've been talking about God all this time—what has it brought us except the Dark Ages? Now it's the age of light, the Enlightenment, and light will come through the study of science." Where there was once a focus on God in pre-modern Europe, modern Europe became focused on studying the world, the physical universe. Practically speaking, the universe replaced God as the center of human concern.

When the first element of the unseen—God Himself—was removed from people's focus, which life would they worry about? The life to come in the unseen that God promised, or the life they have right now? The entire focus becomes this life. You no longer reflect about the afterlife because, to the materialist mind, "What fantasy world are you talking about? What paradise? Why would I even wonder about that? I'm a physical being with physical needs, and those needs are being provided by the physical universe."

Where God was replaced by the universe, the afterlife (al-Akhirah) was replaced by this worldly life (ad-dunya). The Christian focus on the afterlife had made worldly life miserable—they were killing people through the Spanish Inquisition, torturing even Christians by accusing them of being Jews, which they considered the worst possible accusation. Post-revolutionary Europe rejected anyone who believes in the afterlife, assuming they must want to make the world a miserable place.

The Christians used to say, "You may be suffering in your body, but I'm trying to save your soul." Post-modernity responded: "What soul? I don't see a soul. I've done a scan of my body—all I see is blood, skin and bone. No CAT scan has revealed any soul. You mean my feelings and emotions? Those are just neurons firing, just chemicals we can research. There is no soul." Psychology courses teach about how Christians used to perform exorcisms, claiming a demon had taken over someone's body and soul—they would literally drill a hole in the person's skull to let the demon out. Skulls with drilled holes have been found as archaeological evidence.

You can imagine how people developed an aversion to the idea of "helping your soul." God was replaced with the universe. The afterlife was replaced with this life. The soul was replaced with the body—take care of your body through nutrition, working out, living well, comfort, luxury. This became the "standard of living."

This is all a consequence of Christian faith. This new materialism—an obsession with the material world, the universe, this life, the body—all stems from that original deviation. This Surah teaches us that the actual consequence of Christian faith was globalized materialism. It became implanted deep into the human psyche. Even when you talk about the Akhirah to a Muslim today, they might think in the back of their head, "Yeah, yeah, I know," and dismiss it subconsciously. They've internalized the ideas of materialism deep inside.

This is why Allah ﷻ says إِن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ—if they don't come to believe in this speech, meaning the Quran. Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say "this Quran" (هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْآن)—He said "this speech" (هَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ). There's a beautiful gem here: the Surah started with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ الْكِتَابَ (All praise is for Allah who has sent down upon His slave the Book), but didn't say "believe in this Book." Instead, it says believe in this حَدِيث (speech, conversation).

The word حَدِيث means speech. The Quran will remain alive by means of speech—it must be spoken. People must converse it, discuss it. It must become living discourse, an ongoing conversation. The Prophet ﷺ might even be worried, reading between the lines, that Muslims won't speak the Quran anymore, won't make it part of their active discourse.

The ayah ends with أَسَفًا—out of grief. Asaf refers to sadness in a situation where you don't have any control, where the matter is out of your hands. The Prophet ﷺ recognized that the real consequences of Christian faith were beyond his control, because he wouldn't be around to witness them. But Allah ﷻ had already guaranteed the one thing that would remain standing without deviation, holding the believers up: the Quran.

translation: Indeed, We have made that which is on the earth adornment for it that We may test them [as to] which of them is best in deed
commentary:
**إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا**

*Indeed, We have made that which is on the earth adornment for it that We may test them [as to] which of them is best in deed.*

Look at how directly relevant this conversation is to materialism. Allah ﷻ says: We have no doubt made whatever is on the earth as a means of beautifying it. The world is beautiful in its treasures and riches. Who knew that small speckles of dirt, of sand, silicone, could transform the planet? Who knew that computer chips would come from silicon? Who knew that copper cables would revolutionize how the global economy functions?

The means by which we find beauty in the world—the devices we use, the technologies we play with—all their ingredients were already on the earth. Allah ﷻ made them as a means of beautifying the world, but for a specific purpose: لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ—to test them. أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا—which of them is best in terms of action.

Notice that from the beginning to the end of this Surah, Allah ﷻ keeps highlighting action, deeds, work. When the Surah comes to its conclusion, we'll see another conversation about action. The message is clear: get to work. Who's going to be better in terms of their deeds?

translation: And indeed, We will make that which is upon it [into] a barren ground
commentary:
**وَإِنَّا لَجَـٰعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا**

*And indeed, We will make that which is upon it [into] a barren ground.*

There is very powerful imagery in this ayah. وَإِنَّا لَجَـٰعِلُونَ—We are no doubt going to transform, We are going to make. The particle إِنَّ already emphasizes certainty, and the لَام adds an oath to it: no doubt about it, I swear to it. We are going to make مَا عَلَيْهَا—whatever is on this earth, which in the previous ayah was described as something made for the purpose of beauty.

Now Allah ﷻ will take whatever is on it and turn it into صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا. The word صَعِيد comes from صَعِد, which means to climb or ascend. But صَعِيد also means dirt, dust gathered together—the kind you see when something has been sitting somewhere for a long time. Like a desolate town, a piece of furniture you haven't cleaned in years, or a car sitting in the garage that you approach after three or four years to find covered in dust.

Allah ﷻ is saying: whatever is on the earth will be covered in layers upon layers of dust. It's going to be dead, covered and buried in dust. 

جُرُز literally is one of the words for قَحْط (drought, barrenness). It means cut off from all life—cut-up, dry land, barren land where nothing can grow. The earth will be entirely lifeless. No vegetation, no plants, nothing will grow. صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا—completely barren and covered in dust.

This is what's going to happen to this beautified world. This is the introduction to the Surah, the premise under which we study everything that follows. The Surah will teach us four main stories, and between some of them, there will be commentary from Allah ﷻ before moving to the next story. But this opening is critical because it sets the stage, providing the perspective from which we should view all these narratives.

translation: Or have you thought that the companions of the cave and the inscription were, among Our signs, a wonder
commentary:
**أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْكَهْفِ وَٱلرَّقِيمِ كَانُوا۟ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا عَجَبًا**

*Or have you thought that the companions of the cave and the inscription were, among Our signs, a wonder?*

Someone who believes only in this world and its beauty should pay extra attention to the people of the cave. أَمْ حَسِبْتَ—Have you ever assumed? Has it ever captured your imagination? أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْكَهْفِ—the companions of the cave, the people of the cave.

وَٱلرَّقِيمِ—and ar-Raqim. This word is close to مَرْقُوم, like in كِتَابٌ مَّرْقُومٌ (an inscribed book). رَقْم means to inscribe something in a way that's hard to erase—it could be soldered in, stitched in, or carved into rock. When ancient historians would document something permanently, they would carve it into stone, etching it in a way that couldn't be erased. This is also called رَقْم.

Instead of using مَرْقُوم, Allah ﷻ uses رَقِيم to emphasize that it lasted an extremely long time, because the pattern فَعِيل often indicates permanence. As the story unfolds, we'll learn about a carving that was made—an inscription that lasted for centuries without the data on it being erased.

كَانُوا۟ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا عَجَبًا—Did you assume that they themselves were, from among Our miraculous signs, something strange? Something uniquely wondrous? The word عَجَب carries the meaning of something so remarkable, so extraordinary, that it stands out as unusual and amazing.

This question opens the door to the first major story of the Surah—the story of the companions of the cave, a story that will directly address the themes of materialism, faith and the true nature of this worldly life that have been established in these opening ayahs.

translation: [Mention] when the youths retreated to the cave and said, "Our Lord, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance
commentary:
إِذْ أَوَى ٱلْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا

The word أَوَى in Arabic means to join with something for protection. Picture a child who gets in trouble and runs to hide behind their mother, holding onto her for safety. That captures the essence of this word. Allah ﷻ says أَوَى ٱلْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ - when the young people sought refuge in the cave.

Notice that Allah ﷻ uses the specific term الْفِتْيَةُ, meaning young men. Earlier, He referred to them as أَصْحَابُ الْكَهْفِ (the people of the cave), which doesn't specify age. But here, He highlights their youth deliberately. The previous passage of this surah established that the world has been made extremely beautiful - إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا. Who is most susceptible to the temptations of this earth? The youth. Who has the energy, the strength, the vitality to fully consume the pleasures and entertainment this world offers? Young people.

When you see a group of young men hanging out together, they're typically enjoying the beauty of this life - going out to eat, playing sports, watching movies, joking around. If they're believers, perhaps they're doing something beneficial. If not, they might be going to clubs or engaging in worse activities. The point is that young men, by nature, are drawn to the excitement of worldly life.

Yet here, these young men made the opposite choice. They were living in a society of luxury, but they decided to leave everything behind because of their faith. According to several scholars, this story took place after Isa (AS) left this world. The Romans had crushed a Jewish uprising, leading to the second destruction in 70 CE. A handful of followers of Isa (AS) remained, and these young men are believed to have been among them.

When it was discovered that they were believers, they were brought to trial. The king confronted them directly: are you believers or not? Because they were young, they were given a deadline - something like being released on bail with the condition that they couldn't leave town. Their names were recorded, and they were told to return within a specified time having renounced their faith, or face execution.

With only a few of them against an entire empire, they couldn't fight. Their only option was to flee. They ran to the mountains and found a cave where they could hide. As they were escaping, running for their lives, they made this profound dua: رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً - "Our Master, grant us mercy especially from You."

The phrase مِن لَّدُنكَ means "especially from You" or "from Your own self." This is significant because earlier in the surah, we saw that tribulation came مِن لَّدُنَّا - especially from Allah ﷻ. Now, reading between the lines, Allah ﷻ is teaching us something beautiful: if the trial is going to be especially from Him, then you should ask for a mercy that is especially from Him.

They continue: وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا - "And provide for us from our affair right guidance." The word هَيِّئْ comes from هَيْئَة, which means to create a shape or form. Figuratively, it means to provide the circumstances, to make things happen, to hook someone up with what they need. May Allah ﷻ make it happen for you and make it easy - this is the essence of the word.

So they're asking: provide for us the means, create the situation for us, open up the way for us regarding our decision - meaning their choice to flee and protect their faith. They want guidance in both the figurative and literal sense. Figuratively, they need spiritual guidance to stay firm. Literally, they need to find a safe place to hide. "Make it easy for us to escape and find refuge."

translation: So We cast [a cover of sleep] over their ears within the cave for a number of years
commentary:
فَضَرَبْنَا عَلَىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ فِى ٱلْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَدًا

Allah ﷻ responds to their dua immediately: فَضَرَبْنَا عَلَىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ - "We struck upon their ears." When someone gets hit hard on their ears, they lose their ability to hear. This became an Arabic expression - to say someone was "struck on the ear" means they can't hear anymore, they've been knocked out.

In the previous surah, we learned that a burden can be placed on the ear. But here, Allah ﷻ uses even stronger language - He struck their ears, meaning He completely sealed their hearing. Why their ears specifically? Because if you're going to sleep in a cave, what can wake you up? Noise. And they didn't have noise-canceling headphones or earplugs. So Allah ﷻ plugged their ears Himself, ensuring they would remain in deep sleep.

سِنِينَ عَدَدًا - "for a number of years." The word عَدَدًا means a specific number, but Allah ﷻ doesn't tell us how many. And this ambiguity itself contains a powerful lesson. One of the flaws that afflicted earlier communities was their obsession with nitpicking irrelevant details. It's beautiful to examine the Quran carefully, to derive every gem and treasure we can from it for our guidance. But there are some details that simply won't benefit you.

When Yusuf (AS) was in prison, does the Quran tell us the name of the prisoner he spoke to? Does it tell us the minister's name, or his wife's name? We may know these things from other sources, but the Quran doesn't mention them because they're irrelevant to our guidance. Which brother suggested killing Yusuf (AS) - was it the youngest or the oldest? The Quran doesn't say, because it doesn't matter for your spiritual growth.

Allah ﷻ leaves certain things ambiguous because they have nothing to do with guidance. Everything you need to know, Allah ﷻ has clarified - تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ - a clear explanation of all things. All things relevant, that is.

translation: Then We awakened them that We might show which of the two factions was most precise in calculating what [extent] they had remained in time
commentary:
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَىُّ ٱلْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصَىٰ لِمَا لَبِثُوٓا۟ أَمَدًا

ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ - "Then We raised them" - We woke them up after all those years. لِنَعْلَمَ أَىُّ ٱلْحِزْبَيْنِ - "so We may know which of the two groups."

The word حِزْب is particularly meaningful here. It refers to a group, but not just any group - it specifically means a group that is harsh or tough with each other. It can refer to a faction of an army because they're battle-hardened. It means a like-minded group who all think alike, united under a particular banner. These are all nuances of the word hizb.

Why is this word used here? Because when they woke up after however many years they had been asleep, they had no idea how long they'd been out. One of them says, "We must have been here for a few hours." Another says, "No way, it was a couple of days." The first insists, "Come on, it was only four hours!" And because they're friends, they got a little tough with each other and broke into two groups, each arguing their position. "What day is it? Thursday or Monday?" They're going at it with each other.

This is actually a beautiful, realistic touch. Young men can break into an argument so easily. They could be sitting around peacefully discussing religion, and suddenly someone brings up a controversial topic and it gets heated out of nowhere. "You don't know what you're talking about!" The discussion escalates into a full argument.

أَحْصَىٰ لِمَا لَبِثُوٓا۟ أَمَدًا - "which group had better counted the duration they had remained." The word أَحْصَىٰ comes from حَصَاة, which means a pebble. The Arabs didn't have sophisticated calculators, so they used pebbles as their counting mechanism. From this, ahsa came to mean not just counting, but accounting - keeping a precise record. So the question is: which of the two groups can count exactly and keep the most accurate record of how long they remained?

The word أَمَدًا refers to something prolonged - a long distance or a long time. Despite their disagreement about the exact duration, both groups acknowledged one thing: they'd been out for a while. That much they all agreed upon.

translation: It is We who relate to you, [O Muhammad], their story in truth. Indeed, they were youths who believed in their Lord, and We increased them in guidance
commentary:
نَّحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ وَزِدْنَـٰهُمْ هُدًى

Here Allah ﷻ pauses the narrative to make a special interjection. This is one of the beautiful literary features of the Quran called iltifat - you're in the middle of a story, and suddenly you take a step back to make a crucial point. It's like when a teacher is explaining a lesson and reaches a particular line that reminds him of something important. He takes off his glasses, takes a sip of water, sits back and says, "Before I go on, you need to know something really special about this point."

نَّحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ - "We are narrating to you their story with truth." This is significant because the Jews had asked about the People of the Cave, and the Christians also had their version. So Allah ﷻ is establishing two things: First, this narration is truthful. Second, it's being told to you بِٱلْحَقِّ - with purpose, with truth, with what is fundamentally right.

The purpose of this story has already been outlined at the beginning of the surah. Everything you're being told is tied to that purpose - the conflict between concern for the Hereafter versus attachment to worldly life, the battle against materialism. Even this story about the People of the Cave connects to that central theme.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ - "Without doubt, they were young people." He could have simply continued the story, but He stops to emphasize their youth again. Why? Because Allah ﷻ wants to highlight something remarkable: these were young people, and yet ءَامَنُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ - they truly believed in their Master.

This entire emphasis on their youth speaks to the potential of young people in times of trial. We often think of youth as a time of weakness, of being easily tempted. We focus on "the problems of the youth." But Allah ﷻ is showing us that the people who will prefer the discomfort of a cave over the luxury of life, who will sacrifice everything to hold onto their religion, are going to be young people. The real flag-bearers of faith, the people of true sacrifice, will be the youth.

This ayah is like a wake-up call: Come on, young people, wake up! Just as those youth woke up in the cave, we who are sleeping need to wake up too.

وَزِدْنَـٰهُمْ هُدًى - "And We increased them in guidance." When they demonstrated their iman, when they showed they were willing to sacrifice for their faith, Allah ﷻ decided He would open a way out for them. This is the beautiful reality of divine guidance building upon human effort. He showed them the way to the mountains. He guided them to the right mountain, to the right cave. This is called ilham - divine inspiration.

Allah ﷻ puts a feeling in your heart: we should take a left here, we should turn right there, yes this is the right spot, we should stay here. Even their decision to bring a dog with them - all of this is ilham. And sometimes, when you decide you're going to do something for the sake of Allah ﷻ, when you're truly committed to holding onto your faith, then Allah ﷻ will place something inside you. He'll inspire you to start something, to do something, to take action, to make a move, to pursue that opportunity. The bulk of the success comes from Allah ﷻ, and He will see your project through.

translation: And We made firm their hearts when they stood up and said, "Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. Never will we invoke besides Him any deity. We would have certainly spoken, then, an excessive transgression
commentary:
وَرَبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ إِذْ قَامُوا۟ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَن نَّدْعُوَا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَـٰهًا ۖ لَّقَدْ قُلْنَآ إِذًا شَطَطًا

Now the timeline shifts back. This ayah takes us to when they were standing before the king, when he confronted them about their faith.

وَرَبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ - "And We tied up their hearts" or "We put firmness on their hearts." This phrase appears in the Quran in situations where, emotionally, a person wouldn't normally be able to carry themselves without divine intervention. It was used, for example, when the mother of Musa (AS) put him in the water - لَوْلَا أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا - if We hadn't strengthened her heart, she wouldn't have been able to handle it emotionally.

Is it easy for young people to stand trial surrounded by soldiers, courtiers and judges? It's a nerve-racking, terrifying situation. Young people can be brave, but they can also be very easily intimidated. They could have caved under pressure, but Allah ﷻ gave them a strength of heart they could not have possessed on their own.

Notice that the subject of the verb isn't them - it's Allah ﷻ. They didn't strengthen their own hearts. We strengthened their hearts. We made their hearts firm. This is a gift Allah ﷻ gives as a result of faith. The believers had exhibited good character before, and at the critical moment, Allah ﷻ granted them a strength they couldn't have mustered themselves.

إِذْ قَامُوا۟ - "When they stood." The mention of standing is significant because they stood up to the king. This is an act of bravery. They didn't cower or remain seated - they stood in defiance of tyranny.

فَقَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - "And they said: Our Master is the Master of the heavens and the earth." This is their testimony, their declaration of faith before a tyrant.

لَن نَّدْعُوَا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَـٰهًا - "We will never call upon any deity besides Him." They're making their position absolutely clear. There's no ambiguity, no room for compromise.

لَّقَدْ قُلْنَآ إِذًا شَطَطًا - "If we did that, we would have certainly spoken an outrageous lie." The word شَطَطًا doesn't just mean a regular lie or exaggeration. It means going way beyond the limits, making up something so crazy and far-fetched that it's completely detached from truth. It's like saying a donkey has two horns - it makes absolutely no sense.

So they publicly declared that the religion the king was demanding they accept is so out of whack, so completely wrong, that it doesn't even merit consideration. It's not even within the vicinity of truth. This is a bold, fearless statement to make before a tyrant who has the power to execute them.

translation: These, our people, have taken besides Him deities. Why do they not bring for [worship of] them a clear authority? And who is more unjust than one who invents about Allah a lie
commentary:
هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمُنَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ لَّوْلَا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بَيِّنٍ ۖ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا

But they didn't stop there. They went even further.

هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمُنَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً - "These people of ours have taken gods besides Him." They're not just defending their own faith - they're going on the offensive, criticizing the king's religion and the religion of their nation.

لَّوْلَا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بَيِّنٍ - "Why don't they bring concerning them a clear authority?" In other words, put them on trial! Why don't you demand proof from them? Where's their evidence? The word سُلْطَان means authoritative proof, a clear demonstration. They're essentially saying: if you're going to put us on trial for our beliefs, why not put your own religion on trial? Let's see your evidence!

فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا - "And who is more wrong than one who invents a lie about Allah?" Here's where they really cross the line. The king thinks they're the criminals, but they flip it: who's the real criminal here? Who's the worst wrongdoer? The one who fabricates lies about Allah ﷻ!

They're standing before the king, and they're essentially calling him and his entire nation criminals. They're saying: you think we're guilty? You're the guilty ones. You're committing the worst crime - attributing lies to Allah ﷻ.

The king responded by telling them they were young, that they were running their mouths. He recognized they were juveniles, so he gave them a week to think it through. But he made it clear that what they'd already said was enough for a death sentence. If they wanted to come back and apologize, that was another matter. Otherwise, they would be executed.

translation: [The youths said to one another], "And when you have withdrawn from them and that which they worship other than Allah, retreat to the cave. Your Lord will spread out for you of His mercy and will prepare for you from your affair facility
commentary:
وَإِذِ ٱعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ فَأْوُۥٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ يَنشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَيُهَيِّئْ لَكُم مِّنْ أَمْرِكُم مِّرْفَقًا

وَإِذِ ٱعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ - "And when you have withdrawn from them." The word ٱعْتَزَلْ in Arabic means to take a side step, to avoid something, to detach yourself from something, to be alone. It means to cut yourself away from the crowd. Like when you're at a gathering and everyone's sitting together, but you find some corner to sit by yourself or you go outside to make a phone call - that's i'tizal. You've separated yourself from the group.

وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ - "And what they worship other than Allah." So when you've separated yourselves from them and from their false worship...

فَأْوُۥٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ - "Then retreat to the cave." Here we see the same word أَوَى that appeared in ayah 10 - seek refuge, take shelter. We're not explicitly told who is speaking here. It could be one of the more intelligent members of their group giving advice to the others. It may even be that Allah ﷻ placed this instruction directly in their hearts, just as He placed guidance in the heart of Musa's mother.

يَنشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ - "Your Master will spread out for you from His mercy." The word يَنشُرْ means to expand, to spread out, to open up. This is profound because a cave is supposed to be a place of confinement - it's tight, it's enclosed. But Allah ﷻ uses the opposite word. He'll expand for you, He'll open up for you.

There's a beautiful contrast here. The cave may feel physically tight and confining, but don't think of it as constriction. This is going to be an opening of mercy. What seems like limitation will actually be liberation. What appears to be imprisonment will be protection.

وَيُهَيِّئْ لَكُم مِّنْ أَمْرِكُم مِّرْفَقًا - "And He will prepare for you from your affair a means of ease." The word مِّرْفَقًا comes from the root meaning to recline, to relax. A mirfaq is something that makes you comfortable - like a pillow, or a friend around whom you can be at ease, someone in whose presence you feel safe and unthreatened.

So Allah ﷻ is promising them: from your situation, from this difficult decision you've made, He will create for you a comfortable resting place. You won't have to worry. The cave won't be a prison - it will be a sanctuary.

And with that, we see their story beginning to unfold, built on the foundation of their courageous faith and Allah's infinite mercy.

translation: And [had you been present], you would see the sun when it rose, inclining away from their cave on the right, and when it set, passing away from them on the left, while they were [laying] within an open space thereof. That was from the signs of Allah. He whom Allah guides is the [rightly] guided, but he whom He leaves astray - never will you find for him a protecting guide
commentary:
**۞ وَتَرَى ٱلشَّمْسَ إِذَا طَلَعَت تَّزَٰوَرُ عَن كَهْفِهِمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَإِذَا غَرَبَت تَّقْرِضُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ وَهُمْ فِى فَجْوَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ وَلِيًّا مُّرْشِدًا**

Allah ﷻ paints a beautiful scene for us: "And you will see the sun." When Allah ﷻ tells a story, He expects us to visualize it. Nobody was there watching what happened, but He wants us to imagine the scene unfolding.

The word تَّزَٰوَرُ is fascinating. It comes from the root zoor, which means deviation or crookedness in speech. This is actually the second word for crookedness we've encountered in this surah. The first was 'iwaj relating to language, but zoor means to sidestep, to curve out. Think of how light travels in a straight path - the ayah is telling us that when sunlight would enter the cave, it would bend and curve.

We can imagine - and Allah ﷻ knows best how it actually played out - perhaps the cave had an opening at the top, or maybe the rocks around the entrance were positioned in such a way that they reflected the sunlight. The light wouldn't come straight in; it would reflect and provide indirect illumination. This meant the young believers would get light, but it wouldn't hit them directly. The sunlight would circumvent them, staying to their sides rather than exposing them fully.

The ayah continues: when the sun rises, the light comes ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ (on the right side), and when it sets تَّقْرِضُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ (it avoids them on the left side). Notice that the avoidance is mentioned more emphatically in the evening. The word تَّقْرِضُهُمْ means to cut away from, to avoid completely - like someone who's tipsy sidestepping obstacles while walking, or like a driver who sees a checkpoint ahead and takes a detour three blocks away to come back on the road later.

Why would the sun avoid them more in the evening? There's wisdom here. Fewer people travel to the mountains early in the morning. But by evening, if someone went on a hike, they'd be reaching those higher elevations. So there's a greater chance of the believers being discovered in the evening, and Allah ﷻ provided more protection at that time.

The positioning is perfect: وَهُمْ فِى فَجْوَةٍ مِّنْهُ - they're in a fajwah, a small opening or enclave within the cave. A fajwah is like a smaller version of a fajj (valley between mountains) - any small opening between rock formations.

The sunlight coming in serves multiple purposes. It provides warmth without burning heat. It prevents the cave from becoming completely moldy and stuffy, keeping the air fresh. Yet it never exposes them directly. ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - this is from the miraculous signs of Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement about guidance: مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ - whoever Allah ﷻ guides, then he is truly guided. This works on two levels. Figuratively, it means guidance in life, in faith. But literally, it also means Allah ﷻ guided them to this cave and guided them to sleep in exactly the right spot, so that even when light entered the cave, it fell on either side of them without disturbing them.

وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ وَلِيًّا مُّرْشِدًا - and whoever He allows to go astray, you won't find any protective friend for them, nor any guide. Murshid specifically means someone who guides you through a valley or through a difficult journey. If Allah ﷻ isn't your guide, you won't find any guide at all.

translation: And you would think them awake, while they were asleep. And We turned them to the right and to the left, while their dog stretched his forelegs at the entrance. If you had looked at them, you would have turned from them in flight and been filled by them with terror
commentary:
**وَتَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقَاظًا وَهُمْ رُقُودٌ ۚ وَنُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ ۖ وَكَلْبُهُم بَـٰسِطٌ ذِرَاعَيْهِ بِٱلْوَصِيدِ ۚ لَوِ ٱطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِرَارًا وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْبًا**

Now Allah ﷻ describes an incredible scene: if you could peek inside the cave, you would think they're sitting up awake, but they're actually in a deep sleep. The word رُقُودٌ doesn't mean a hard, uncomfortable sleep - it means a light, enjoyable sleep. You know the difference between how someone looks when they're passed out versus when they're taking a peaceful nap? A light sleep almost has a smile on the person's face. It's restful and peaceful. That's ruqood.

وَنُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ - "And We turn them to the right and to the left." Some scholars have interpreted this to mean that Allah ﷻ would physically move them out of the way of sunlight as it shifted position. Others understand it more practically: like patients who are paralyzed and need to be turned regularly to maintain blood flow and prevent bedsores, Allah ﷻ was taking care of these believers even in their sleep. He was making sure they didn't wake up with aches and pains. This is divine care at the most intimate level.

Then comes a remarkable detail: وَكَلْبُهُم بَـٰسِطٌ ذِرَاعَيْهِ بِٱلْوَصِيدِ - "And their dog stretching out its paws at the entrance." Wait - the Quran talks about dogs? This isn't even the first time; it mentioned hunting dogs before. Some people become extreme when something is prohibited, as if we should make that creature extinct from the planet. But Islam doesn't teach us to hate Allah's creation. We don't eat pork, but that doesn't mean we hate pigs. They're part of Allah's creation. Just because something isn't permissible to eat doesn't make it evil or despicable in itself.

So these young believers had a dog with them. Picture the scene: there's the cave with light coming in at an angle. The light hits a certain point and stops. Right at that boundary, right behind where the light reaches, is the dog. And all you can see are its massive paws stretched out - just the paws catching the sunlight.

The word وَصِيد is brilliant. You'd expect the word to be فَتْحَة (opening), but waseed actually means "closing." The entrance looks more closed than open. It appears as if rocks have fallen and blocked the cave entrance. This takes away the curiosity of any climber who might want to explore. Just that one word - waseed - indicates the entrance looks sealed shut.

But imagine there's a really curious climber who starts scaling those rocks to take a peek. The word ٱطَّلَعْتَ comes from tulu' - to rise up, to climb up to discover something. So he climbs and climbs, finally gets on top, and peeks inside. What does he see? Those massive paws. He doesn't know if it's a lion, a wolf, or what - all he knows is that looks like death.

لَوِ ٱطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِرَارًا - If you peeked at them, you would turn away from them running as fast as you can. You wouldn't even look back. وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْبًا - and you would be filled with terror from all of them. Notice: "from all of them" - even though you haven't even seen the people yet! Just the sight of those paws is enough to terrify.

Allah ﷻ has created three layers of protection. First deterrent: the entrance looks closed. Second deterrent: if you somehow get curious and peek, you see those terrifying paws. Third deterrent: even if you're one of those thrill-seekers who gets past the dog and actually sees the people, they look awake. They look like they're sitting up, conscious, watching. That's it - you're running. You would be filled with absolute terror from all of them together - the people and the dog.

translation: And similarly, We awakened them that they might question one another. Said a speaker from among them, "How long have you remained [here]?" They said, "We have remained a day or part of a day." They said, "Your Lord is most knowing of how long you remained. So send one of you with this silver coin of yours to the city and let him look to which is the best of food and bring you provision from it and let him be cautious. And let no one be aware of you
commentary:
**وَكَذَٰلِكَ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَتَسَآءَلُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثْتُمْ فَٱبْعَثُوٓا۟ أَحَدَكُم بِوَرِقِكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ فَلْيَنظُرْ أَيُّهَآ أَزْكَىٰ طَعَامًا فَلْيَأْتِكُم بِرِزْقٍ مِّنْهُ وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ وَلَا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَدًا**

وَكَذَٰلِكَ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ - "And in that way We raised them up." They wake up and start asking each other questions. Now, notice the pronouns shifting in this conversation - this gives us hints about how many people we're dealing with.

قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ - One speaker from among them said. That's one person. كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ - "How long have you all been here?" The "you all" indicates he's talking to at least two others.

قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ - "They said: We remained a day or part of a day." Now we have qaaloo - "they said" - indicating at least three people responding as a group.

But then قَالُوا۟ again - another "they said." This suggests a second group responding. "Your Master knows best how long you remained." There's a hint here of the traditional number: perhaps seven people total, though it's subtle. The Quran is showing us the conversation without being explicit about the exact count.

The second group's response is beautiful: "Your Master knows best how long we've been here - who cares about that? We've been here long enough to build an appetite!" They shift the focus immediately to what matters: فَٱبْعَثُوٓا۟ أَحَدَكُم بِوَرِقِكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ - "Send one of you with this money of yours to the city."

The word وَرِق is beautiful - it literally means "paper" or "leaf," and it refers to silver currency. They're telling one person to take the cash and go to the city.

But listen to their priorities: فَلْيَنظُرْ أَيُّهَآ أَزْكَىٰ طَعَامًا - "Let him see which is purest in terms of food." You'd think when they send someone out, the first warning would be "Watch out for the authorities! We're fugitives!" But no - the first instruction is to make sure the food is pure, halal, good. These are young people who just woke up starving, and their first concern is still the purity of what they eat. It's exactly like young people today gathering for a sleepover - in the morning someone says, "Yo, who's going to get food?" And everyone responds: "Make sure it's halal!"

فَلْيَأْتِكُم بِرِزْقٍ مِّنْهُ - "Then let him bring you provision from it." Notice: provision "from it" - min-hu. Not "bring all of it." Don't go to a restaurant and order everything on the menu just because you have money. When you're really hungry, your brain doesn't work properly. You order food for eight people when you're only three. So they're saying: get some food, a portion of it, don't go crazy.

وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ - "And let him be subtle, careful, gentle." The word لطف (lutf) means subtlety, to take it easy, act normal, blend in. Be aware of your surroundings. Watch for any subtle movements that might indicate danger. Just watch your back. Don't walk down there looking suspicious like a criminal. Be casual.

وَلَا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَدًا - "And let not anyone become aware of you." Not just "you" singular - the one going - but "you" plural, all of us. We all need to be careful now.

Why such caution? These young men are about to discover they've been in that cave for 300 years. Fashion has changed. Currency has changed. Language has changed. And fashion changes most dramatically with young people. When you see an old man dressed in outdated clothes, nobody really notices. But when you see a young person dressed in the fashion of three centuries ago, it sticks out immediately. An older person can wear their grandfather's suit and it's fine. But a young person? That's going to draw attention real quick.

This is the beginning of their emergence back into a world that has moved on without them - a world where everything they knew has transformed, where they'll need to navigate carefully to understand what Allah ﷻ has decreed for them in this miraculous preservation and awakening.

translation: Indeed, if they come to know of you, they will stone you or return you to their religion. And never would you succeed, then - ever
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ إِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِى مِلَّتِهِمْ وَلَن تُفْلِحُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا

The young believer who was sent down to the city with the old currency returns with an urgent warning inscribed on the note. He warns his companions: "If they overcome you, they will stone you to death" - يَرْجُمُوكُمْ. The gravity of their situation becomes clear. These youth face two stark options, neither of which they can accept.

The second threat follows immediately: "Or they will re-assimilate you back into their nation" - أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِى مِلَّتِهِمْ. Notice that Allah ﷻ uses the word مِلَّة (nation) rather than دِين (religion). This distinction reveals something profound about the society these young men fled. Their people had confused religious identity with national identity. Leaving the state religion was viewed as an act of treason against the country itself. The religious establishment had become so intertwined with political power that to abandon one was to betray the other.

This same confusion manifests in societies throughout history and continues today. When religion becomes primarily a marker of cultural or national identity rather than a path to truth and submission to Allah ﷻ, it creates tremendous pressure on those who want to practice their faith authentically.

The two threats represent the dual dangers facing committed youth in any age. The first is outright persecution - standing firm on your principles might cost you everything, even your life. The second is more subtle but perhaps more dangerous: gradual assimilation. Just blend in a little. Don't be so rigid. You can keep some of your beliefs privately, but publicly, just be like everyone else. The promise seems harmless at first, but the warning that follows is severe: وَلَن تُفْلِحُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا - "And you will never succeed in that case, ever."

The word أَبَدًا (ever, eternally) emphasizes the permanence of this spiritual death. Physical death through stoning would be tragic, but spiritual death through assimilation is catastrophic. This speaks to the experience many young Muslims face today. You look at people who were once more committed, more active in the deen, and you barely recognize them anymore. What happened? Reality hit. They found different friends. They learned to just blend in. But in the process of fitting in, they lost something essential. Their deen became a casualty of their desire to belong.

This doesn't mean Muslims cannot participate in their societies. The companions of the cave themselves were dressed like everyone else when they first left their city. They would have blended in physically. It wasn't their clothing or their cultural habits that set them apart - it was their character and their refusal to compromise on matters of faith. There are aspects of any society that are neutral or even good, aspects we can and should embrace. The danger lies in compromising our core values and allegiances. When we assimilate in matters of millah - when our primary allegiance shifts from Allah ﷻ to the approval of society - that's when we lose the ability to succeed in our ultimate purpose.

translation: And similarly, We caused them to be found that they [who found them] would know that the promise of Allah is truth and that of the Hour there is no doubt. [That was] when they disputed among themselves about their affair and [then] said, "Construct over them a structure. Their Lord is most knowing about them." Said those who prevailed in the matter, "We will surely take [for ourselves] over them a masjid
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَعْثَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَآ إِذْ يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ ۖ فَقَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم بُنْيَـٰنًا ۖ رَّبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِم مَّسْجِدًا

Allah ﷻ says, "And that is how We made them slip" - وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَعْثَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ. The word أَعْثَرْنَا comes from the root meaning to stumble or to slip, and figuratively it means to let something slip that reveals a secret you were trying to hide.

Picture the scene: the young man goes down to the city thinking he's being careful. He's concealing the ancient currency, trying not to draw attention to himself. But things look different - people are dressed strangely, the language has evolved, everything feels off. He enters a shop and asks for food, perhaps using archaic expressions that nobody uses anymore. When he pulls out the money with the picture of the long-dead king on it, the shopkeeper is stunned. "Where did you get this? This is an antique! Why are you dressed like that? Why do you talk like that?" 

A crowd gathers. One question leads to another. The secret he tried so carefully to guard comes spilling out. This is what Allah ﷻ means by "We made them slip." Their discovery wasn't accidental - it was divinely orchestrated.

But why? What was the purpose? لِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ - "So that they could know that the promise of Allah is true." Remember, this is still fundamentally a story about materialism versus faith in the hereafter. The people of that later generation needed to see with their own eyes that Allah ﷻ has power over life and death, that He can preserve life for centuries and resume it. If He can do this, then His promise of resurrection and the Day of Judgment is undeniably real.

وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَآ - "And that the Hour, there is no doubt in it whatsoever." The miracle of the People of the Cave was meant to strengthen belief in the afterlife. This physical, tangible miracle should have led people to reflect on Allah's ultimate power.

But then something unfortunate happens. إِذْ يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ - "At the very moment when they started pulling at one another in disagreement about their affair." The word يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ suggests they were tugging at each other, pulling in different directions. Instead of focusing on the lesson - the confirmation of resurrection and Allah's power - they got distracted by secondary matters. They took the young men back to the cave, and by this time, the dominant religion had become Christianity. These men were seen as holy, close to the earliest followers of Prophet Isa (AS), even though theological differences hadn't been explored yet.

From what we understand from various sources, before much interaction could occur, the young men died. Their purpose - to serve as a sign for the later generation - had been fulfilled. But now the people faced a question: what do we do with this sacred site?

فَقَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم بُنْيَـٰنًا - "Some said, 'Build a structure over them.'" Build a monument, a memorial, a museum. This is what people do with great historical events - they create physical reminders. رَّبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ - "Their Master knows better about them." Some sensible voices said this isn't about buildings and monuments. Allah ﷻ knows best how they should be honored. The real lesson is to learn from their example, not to get caught up in constructing shrines.

But then: قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِم مَّسْجِدًا - "Those who overpowered the conversation said, 'We will definitely take over them a place of worship.'" The people who were dominant in the discourse insisted on building a masjid in their memory. Their reasoning was seemingly noble: these young men fled to get closer to Allah ﷻ, so let's not lose sight of that purpose. Let's build a place where people can worship and remember why these youth made their sacrifice.

Yet even this well-intentioned response represents a distraction from the main lesson. The point wasn't to sanctify a location or build monuments to holy people. The point was to internalize the lesson about Allah's power, the reality of resurrection, and the importance of holding firm to faith regardless of the cost. When we focus on commemorating the people instead of learning from their example, we miss the forest for the trees.

translation: They will say there were three, the fourth of them being their dog; and they will say there were five, the sixth of them being their dog - guessing at the unseen; and they will say there were seven, and the eighth of them was their dog. Say, [O Muhammad], "My Lord is most knowing of their number. None knows them except a few. So do not argue about them except with an obvious argument and do not inquire about them among [the speculators] from anyone
commentary:
سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ رَّابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ سَادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ رَجْمًۢا بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ وَيَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ وَثَامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِم مَّا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ ۗ فَلَا تُمَارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِرَآءً ظَـٰهِرًا وَلَا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِم مِّنْهُمْ أَحَدًا

The ayah addresses one of the questions the Jews of Madinah asked the Prophet ﷺ to test him: how many were the People of the Cave? Allah ﷻ presents three possibilities that people would speculate about: "They will say they were three, the fourth being their dog. And they say five, the sixth being their dog" - سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ رَّابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ سَادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ.

After mentioning these two opinions, Allah ﷻ immediately dismisses them: رَجْمًۢا بِٱلْغَيْبِ - "pelting stones in the dark." This is a powerful Arabic expression meaning to make wild guesses. If you have stones in your hand and you throw them in the dark, you have no idea what you'll hit. Even if you do hit your target, you won't know it, and if you do, it's pure chance, not skill. These first two opinions are nothing more than baseless speculation.

Then comes the third opinion: وَيَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ وَثَامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ - "And they say seven, and the eighth being their dog." Notice the shift in language. After criticizing the first two opinions, Allah ﷻ mentions this third one differently. He adds emphasis with وَثَامِنُهُمْ (and the eighth of them). The structure of the sentence changed. He gave more weight to this statement. While Allah ﷻ doesn't explicitly confirm it, the linguistic hints suggest this is the correct answer.

But immediately after presenting these opinions, Allah ﷻ teaches us something more important than the answer itself: قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِم - "Say: My Master knows best their number." Even if there are hints pointing to the right answer, the Prophet ﷺ is instructed not to get caught up in debating the details. Your Lord knows best. مَّا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ - "None know them except a few." It's not completely unknowable - some people do know. But the knowledge is limited to very few.

Then comes the instruction: فَلَا تُمَارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِرَآءً ظَـٰهِرًا - "So don't argue about them except in an obvious, clear way." The word تُمَارِ specifically means to argue about something when the conclusion is difficult to accept or when emotions run high. Don't get into heated debates about these details. If you engage at all, do it in a straightforward manner with clear proofs, without getting emotionally invested in winning the argument.

وَلَا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِم مِّنْهُمْ أَحَدًا - "And don't ask any fatwa about them from anyone among them." Don't go to the Jews asking for their opinion about this. They were the ones who posed this question to test you, and they don't actually know the answer themselves. Why would you seek knowledge from people who are using the question to create doubt rather than seeking truth?

This entire passage teaches us a profound lesson about knowledge and debate in Islam. Some details matter deeply - matters of aqeedah, matters of halal and haram, matters that affect how we practice our deen. But other details, while interesting, don't change anything about how we should live our lives. Whether the People of the Cave were five or seven doesn't change the lessons we should draw from their story. Getting caught up in these debates distracts us from the purpose of the narrative. We can become so focused on winning arguments about peripheral details that we miss the transformative message at the heart of the story.

## Ayahs 23-24

وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا

إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَـٰذَا رَشَدًا

After telling the story of young men who thought they would return to the city tomorrow, only to wake up three centuries later, Allah ﷻ teaches us about tomorrow: وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا - "And never say about anything, 'I am definitely going to do that tomorrow.'"

Tomorrow is not under your control. Tomorrow belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. The lesson is directly tied to the story. These young men thought they were planning for the next day, but Allah ﷻ had other plans that spanned centuries. According to some narrations about the context of revelation, the Prophet ﷺ was asked these questions about the People of the Cave by the Jews, and he said he would bring them an answer tomorrow. But he didn't add "insha'Allah" - if Allah wills. When revelation stopped coming temporarily, some people began mocking the Prophet ﷺ. The delay taught a lesson about presuming control over the future.

إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - "Except that Allah wills." Always add this qualifier when you speak about the future. Don't say "I'll definitely do this tomorrow" with absolute certainty. Say "insha'Allah." And if you forget to say it in the moment, you can add it afterward: وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ - "And mention your Master when you forget."

This teaches us something beautiful about the phrase insha'Allah. When you say it, you're not really saying it to the person you're talking to. You're saying it to Allah ﷻ. So if you hang up the phone and realize you forgot to say insha'Allah, you can still say it afterward. "I'll see you at three. Oh - insha'Allah." Even if the other person can't hear you anymore, it doesn't matter. You're acknowledging to Allah ﷻ that the future is in His hands.

And don't be shy about saying insha'Allah in front of people who might not understand. If someone finds it odd that Muslims attach this phrase to future plans, that's okay. It's a profound act of faith to acknowledge that we don't control what happens next. Every statement about the future should be colored by this recognition.

But the ayah goes even further: وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَـٰذَا رَشَدًا - "And say: Perhaps my Master will guide me to something closer than this in terms of guidance." This is remarkable. Not only should you say insha'Allah, recognizing that your plans might not materialize, but you should actively hope that if your plans change, it's because Allah ﷻ is guiding you to something better.

You plan to go somewhere tomorrow, insha'Allah. But you should also say: perhaps Allah ﷻ will guide me to something that brings me even closer to Him than what I'm currently planning. Maybe that restaurant you were planning to visit would have led to an accident. Maybe that job you were pursuing wasn't actually good for you. Maybe Allah ﷻ will send rain and cancel your plans, and that cancellation will be a mercy.

This is also a universal principle beyond just the immediate lesson. All of us are on a journey toward guidance. You learn something about the deen at one point and feel like you're beginning to understand. Then Allah ﷻ introduces you to another teacher, another book, another circle of righteous friends, and you come closer to guidance.
