ومن أظلم ممن ذكر بآيات ربه فأعرض عنها - and who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who was reminded of the ayat of his Master, then ignored them? Who could be worse than someone who heard the Quran, who was given advice and counsel through Allah's words, then turned away from them?

Now, this is directed at the kuffar, at those who reject faith entirely. But we need to pause here. Every Friday, we hear ayat of the Quran in salah and khutbah. These ayat give advice about our lives, guidance for our choices, warnings about our behavior. Are we listening? Or are we ignoring them after the fact? This warning isn't limited to the disbelievers.

ونسي ما قدمت يداه - and he forgot what his own hands had sent forward. First, he ignores the reminder. The opposite of ذِكْر (remembrance) is نِسْيان (forgetfulness). So he forgets the message, and worse, he forgets his own deeds, his own actions, his own mistakes.

Think about what Allah ﷻ is teaching us here: when we're being reminded through the Quran, what helps us truly take that reminder to heart? It's keeping track of our own deeds, our behaviors, our past mistakes - the things we're going to have to answer Allah ﷻ for. When you think about those things, when you honestly assess your own actions, it becomes easier to pay attention to Allah's book. But when you're oblivious to your past, when you think, "Oh, it's all covered, it's all forgiven, no need to worry about what I've done" - that's when you stop caring about what the Quran has to say. You'll only engage with it superficially.

إنا جعلنا على قلوبهم أكنة أن يفقهوه - indeed, We have placed upon their hearts coverings that prevent them from understanding it. We've seen the word أَكِنَّة before - it's a seal, a lock, a covering that prevents light from entering. And we know that revelation is light. So for these people, their hearts are sealed off from the light of understanding.

وفي آذانهم وقرا - and in their ears is a barrier, a filter. Even if they hear, they don't really hear.

But here we must understand something critical: there's a difference between understanding and internalizing. The word فِقْه doesn't just mean understanding - it means deep understanding that has penetrated you, that has become part of you, that you've internalized. You might understand that truth is good, but have you internalized it? You might understand that anger is harmful, but have you deeply absorbed that knowledge so it changes your behavior?

These people might have understanding - they might be able to quote ayat, explain grammar, discuss vocabulary, present scholarly research - but they haven't internalized any of it. It hasn't gone deep. They have knowledge, but it hasn't become part of who they are.

Let me give you an example from within the religious community. Someone gives a lecture or a khutbah. You're listening, and your mind is thinking: "Oh, that was pretty good. If he had added this point or that point, it would have been better. His delivery here could have been smoother." Then you write him an email afterwards: "Pretty good speech, but next time if you add these three points, it'll be even better. Here's a cool quote you could throw in."

That person - do you think adding those points would help them appreciate the message more? No. They're treating the entire act of listening as a performance review, as entertainment to be critiqued. They're listening, but they're not listening. Why do we listen to speeches about the deen? It's not a form of entertainment. If that's the state we've reached, it's truly sad.

Or consider someone taking notes during a lecture - pages upon pages upon pages, an entire encyclopedia in their notebook. But all of this is so they can teach others later. It's not for themselves. They're not taking it into their own heart. My uncle used to say: ما خرج من القلب يصل إلى القلب - what comes from the heart reaches the heart. And what doesn't come from the heart will never reach another heart. If you're not taking knowledge into your heart, you'll never give it to anyone else's heart. It will just be an artificial transfer of information.

This is a truly frightening state to be in, and it's what I fear most for myself and for all of us - that our relationship with revelation would become so shallow, so superficial, so disconnected from our hearts.

وإن تدعهم إلى الهدى فلن يهتدوا إذا أبدا - and if you call them to guidance, they will never be guided, ever. This is the final, devastating consequence. When hearts are sealed, when understanding is blocked, when people engage with revelation without internalizing it - guidance becomes impossible. Not just unlikely, but impossible. فلن يهتدوا - they will never be guided. أبدا - ever.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from becoming people who hear His words but don't truly listen, who understand intellectually but never internalize, who engage with the Quran but never let it penetrate our hearts. May He make us among those who, when reminded by His ayat, take that reminder to heart and let it change us from within.

translation: And your Lord is the Forgiving, full of mercy. If He were to impose blame upon them for what they earned, He would have hastened for them the punishment. Rather, for them is an appointment from which they will never find an escape
commentary:
وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۖ لَوْ يُؤَاخِذُهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ بَل لَّهُم مَّوْعِدٌ لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا

Your Master is الْغَفُورُ, exceedingly forgiving. He is ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ the One who possesses and owns mercy itself. The proof of His forgiveness and mercy is made clear in what follows: if He were to seize people immediately because of what they've earned, then punishment would have been expedited for them right away. But this is not how Allah ﷻ operates. Rather, the reality is that they have an appointed time, a deadline set for them.

The ayah concludes with a powerful image: لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا (they will not find any refuge besides Him). The word مَوْئِل refers to a barn or fenced-off field where shepherds keep camels and sheep safe from wolves and thieves, protected by a good security system. The imagery suggests that when that appointed time comes, people will try to hide like frightened animals, but they won't find any place of safety or escape.

translation: And those cities - We destroyed them when they wronged, and We made for their destruction an appointed time
commentary:
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ وَجَعَلْنَا لِمَهْلِكِهِم مَّوْعِدًا

This ayah extends the warning beyond individuals to entire civilizations. Those towns, those complete societies—We destroyed them when they committed wrong. The word لَمَّا is linguistically significant here. Unlike إِذْ, which simply means "when," lamma carries the meaning of continuation until a final point. It indicates that these nations reached a stage where the only thing that could be said about them was that they had become people of wrongdoing. It wasn't just one deed of wrong, but rather their entire legacy amounted to nothing but oppression and injustice.

The word مَهْلِك combines both time and place of destruction. Allah ﷻ appointed both when and where their destruction would occur. Consider Fir'awn (Pharaoh), who lived in his castle in Egypt, seemingly secure in his power. Yet Allah ﷻ had already set a specific date and place for his end. He wouldn't die in his fortress or on any battlefield. The appointed place was water—a humble death by drowning for one who claimed divinity. This is the meaning of مَوْعِد, the appointed time that cannot be escaped.

translation: And [mention] when Moses said to his servant, "I will not cease [traveling] until I reach the junction of the two seas or continue for a long period
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِفَتَىٰهُ لَآ أَبْرَحُ حَتَىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا

Now we arrive at the third and second-to-last story in the surah. Between the previous stories, we had warnings and reminders. After the story of the people of the cave, there was a brief sermon. Between that story and the tale of the two gardens, another warning appeared. Now, before this third narrative begins, we again find cautionary verses. This structure shows how the surah weaves together storytelling and guidance.

Musa (AS) said to his فَتَى, his young companion and assistant. Some scholars, including Ibn Abbas (RA), identify this young man as Yusha ibn Nun (AS), who would later become a prophet himself. What a remarkable opportunity—to travel with and learn from a messenger of Allah ﷻ. This is the second time in the surah we encounter a fata, a young person in service to knowledge. Between the lines, we learn an important lesson about seeking sacred knowledge: if you cannot find a messenger, find those who carry the message of the messengers. Be in the company of scholars, serve them and learn from them.

Notice that this young man isn't sitting in a formal study circle conducting intensive lessons. He's rowing a boat, helping with the journey, doing whatever needs to be done. The point is to be in the company of knowledge, to serve and observe. When I was young, I sought the company of scholars in whatever capacity I could.

Musa (AS) declares his commitment: لَآ أَبْرَحُ (I will not stop, I will not cease). He is determined and fully committed to this mission. He will continue حَتَّىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ (until I reach the meeting place of the two seas). This refers to two rivers or bodies of water that merge together. From what we understand, there may have been a rock or mountain between them, and as the land narrows, the two waters eventually meet.

Then Musa (AS) adds: أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا (or I will continue for huquba—eons upon eons). According to the most conservative linguistic estimate, a huqub represents an 80-year period, roughly a human lifetime. The plural huquba suggests multiple lifetimes. What Musa (AS) is saying is: "I don't care if I have to spend numerous lifetimes searching. I will keep going until I find that place."

The backstory we learn from hadith literature tells us that Allah ﷻ informed Musa (AS) that there was someone more knowledgeable than him, and that he should seek this person out at the place where two oceans meet. Musa (AS) demonstrates incredible dedication to learning. What makes this even more remarkable is that we already have a teacher-student relationship established between Musa (AS) and his young assistant. Yet here is the teacher himself seeking to become a student. A real teacher is someone who says, "I need a teacher." This is the beautiful dynamic of knowledge in Islam—no matter how much you know, you remain a student, always seeking to learn more.

translation: But when they reached the junction between them, they forgot their fish, and it took its course into the sea, slipping away
commentary:
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِمَا نَسِيَا حُوتَهُمَا فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا

When they both reached the junction between the two bodies of water, something unexpected happened. Notice the dual form نَسِيَا —they both forgot. They both forgot their حُوت, their fish. 

Before we continue, let's understand the context: Yusha (AS) had been given instructions to pack food for the journey, and they took a fish with them. This wasn't a small goldfish—it was a large fish, the kind that could feed travelers. They had already roasted and cooked it because obviously, cooking a fish on a small boat during the journey would be impractical. So they prepared it beforehand.

Now imagine the scene: perhaps Musa (AS) is taking a rest, letting the young man navigate, telling him to alert him if anything significant happens. The young companion is alone with the task of watching and rowing. On a long journey with no scenery, nothing but water, and with his teacher asleep, he has nothing to distract him. In those days, there were no phones, no books to read while traveling, no entertainment. Just the monotony of the journey. He might start singing songs he knows, but eventually even that becomes tiresome. The line between wakefulness and drowsiness, between reality and imagination, begins to blur. You question whether you're truly awake or half-dreaming—anyone who has been on an endless road trip understands this state.

In this state, something miraculous occurred. The fish, which was already cooked and dead, somehow came back to life. فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ (It took its path)—notice the language suggests the fish's course was pre-programmed, as though its biology was designed for this very moment. It took the path it was created to take فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا (in the sea, making a tunnel). 

The word سَرَب literally means a tunnel, and it also evokes the imagery of water in the desert—a mirage. The fish created a wake in the water, like when a speedboat moves quickly and leaves a trail behind it. If you looked at this wake from above, it would appear like an arrow pointing in a specific direction: "That way. That's where you need to go."

The young man saw this incredible sight—a cooked fish coming back to life, jumping into the water, making waves and a clear path. He thought it was amazing, but then, in his state between sleep and waking, he didn't process its significance. Perhaps he thought he had imagined it. So he said nothing, and they kept going.

translation: So when they had passed beyond it, [Moses] said to his boy, "Bring us our morning meal. We have certainly suffered in this, our journey, [much] fatigue
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا قَالَ لِفَتَىٰهُ ءَاتِنَا غَدَآءَنَا لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا

فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا—when they crossed beyond it. The word جَاوَزَ literally means to pass by or cross over. They were supposed to stop when the two bodies of water met. The only sign that occurred at that exact moment was the fish jumping out, which should have been their indicator to stop. But they didn't stop—they kept going, passing beyond the appointed place.

After some time, Musa (AS) woke up and asked his companion how things were going. Everything seemed fine, but then hunger set in. He said, "Bring us our lunch." Then he observed: لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا (We have certainly suffered from this journey of ours, exhaustion). The word نَصَب indicates deep fatigue and weariness.

Some commentators note something significant here: they wouldn't have felt this hunger and exhaustion if they had stopped where they were supposed to stop. The fact that they felt such intense hunger was itself a sign that they had gone beyond their intended destination. The hunger was an alarm that they had missed their mark.

translation: He said, "Did you see when we retired to the rock? Indeed, I forgot [there] the fish. And none made me forget it except Satan - that I should mention it. And it took its course into the sea amazingly
commentary:
قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُۥ ۚ وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا

Now it's the young man's turn to speak. He turns around to get the fish, and suddenly the memory floods back. He turns back to Musa (AS) and says, أَرَءَيْتَ (Did you see)—but obviously Musa (AS) didn't see anything; he was asleep. 

إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ (When we rested by the rock)—فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ (I forgot the fish). Something incredible happened, and he's still not entirely sure whether he dreamed it or if it actually occurred.

Then he immediately offers an excuse: وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ (Nothing made me forget it except Shaytan). He's saying, "It wasn't really my fault—Shaytan made me forget to tell you." Some scholars derive from this that the only real power Shaytan has over us is to make us forget. He can't force us to do evil, but he can make us forget what's important, forget our priorities, forget to mention crucial information.

We can also observe something about human nature here, particularly young people: they're very skilled at making excuses when they mess up. But let's return to something mentioned earlier. In Ayah 61, when the fish jumped out, the Quran said نَسِيَا (they both forgot)—using the dual form. Both of them share responsibility. The first mention of forgetfulness applied to both the leader and his assistant. This teaches us something about leadership and responsibility: if a subordinate makes a mistake, the leader still bears responsibility. An amir must take responsibility for the errors of those under his authority. The frightening implication for teachers is that they are responsible for the failures of their students.

The young man concludes his explanation: وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا (And it took its course into the sea in an amazing way). He calls it عَجَب—something wondrous and strange. After all, a roasted fish coming back to life, jumping into the water and creating waves is extraordinary.

translation: [Moses] said, "That is what we were seeking." So they returned, following their footprints
commentary:
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ ۚ فَٱرْتَدَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا قَصَصًا

Musa (AS) immediately understood the significance. He said, ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ (That is what we were seeking). That strange occurrence with the fish—that was exactly the sign we needed.

فَٱرْتَدَّا (So they returned)—they turned back, retracing their path عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا (upon their footsteps) قَصَصًا (precisely, following the tracks). The word قَصَص comes from the same root as قَصَّ, to trace or follow closely. It's the same word used when the mother of Musa (AS) told her daughter فَقُصِّيهِ (follow him)—meaning trace his steps very carefully, don't lose sight of him. Here too, they traced their steps back with great precision, looking for the exact spot where the miracle occurred.

translation: And they found a servant from among Our servants to whom we had given mercy from us and had taught him from Us a [certain] knowledge
commentary:
فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا

فَوَجَدَا (Then they found) عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ (a slave from among Our slaves). Notice how Allah ﷻ keeps this person's identity anonymous. The Quran doesn't provide a name. Later Islamic literature refers to this figure as al-Khidr, and various opinions exist about his nature. Some say he was a very old man given thousands of years of life, someone who still walks the earth. Others, including some companions like Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA), held the opinion that he was an angel. When people started naming their children "Khidr," Umar (RA) objected, suggesting they shouldn't name children after angels. Other scholars firmly maintained he was human, albeit granted exceptional longevity.

But here's what matters: all you need to know is عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ—a servant from among Our servants. If you start asking too many questions about his identity, you might miss the point of the story entirely. The lesson isn't about who he was, but about what he represents regarding knowledge, wisdom and the limits of human understanding. Let it go and focus on what Allah ﷻ is teaching us through this narrative.

The ayah describes this servant with two qualities: ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا (We had given him mercy from Ourselves especially) وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا (and We had taught him knowledge from Our presence).

Notice the subtle difference in prepositions. The first phrase uses مِنْ عِندِنَا, "from with Us," while the second uses مِن لَّدُنَّا, "from directly from Our presence." The second carries more emphasis, suggesting the knowledge given to him comes even more directly from Allah ﷻ than the mercy. Some scholars speculate about what this special mercy might be—perhaps an extended lifespan, or some other unique blessing. But what's being highlighted most is the extraordinary knowledge he possessed, knowledge that came directly from Allah ﷻ.

translation: Moses said to him, "May I follow you on [the condition] that you teach me from what you have been taught of sound judgement
commentary:
قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا

Now observe the beautiful transition happening in this narrative. Up until this point, we had a clear teacher-student relationship: Musa (AS) as the teacher, and his young companion as the student. But now the roles are about to shift. The teacher is about to become the student. And remember what happened before? The student forgot certain instructions about the fish. Now watch what happens—the teacher who just dealt with his student's forgetfulness is about to forget instructions himself. This is the elegant symmetry of the Quranic narrative.

Musa (AS) approaches with remarkable humility: هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ (Can I follow you?)—may I become your follower, your assistant? Who was following Musa (AS) around before? His fata, his young helper. Now Musa (AS) is asking, "Can I be your student? Do you have an internship available? Could I be your study assistant, your research assistant?"

عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ (On the condition that you teach me). Notice the humility in his language. He could have said تُعَلِّمُنِي, but instead he uses تُعَلِّمَنِ, a shorter, more humble form. It's as if he's saying, "Please teach me," with genuine deference.

مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا (From what you have been taught, of right guidance). He acknowledges that this knowledge comes from Allah ﷻ—"from what you have been taught"—and he seeks rushd, sound guidance and wisdom.

translation: He said, "Indeed, with me you will never be able to have patience
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا

The teacher's response is immediate and firm: إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (Indeed, you will never be able to have patience with me). 

The emphasis is remarkable. He doesn't just say لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ صَبْرًا (you won't be able to be patient). He adds مَعِىَ (with me)—specifically with me. Perhaps you can be patient with other people in other situations, but you won't be able to maintain patience with me. It seems this teacher already knows something about Musa (AS) that Allah ﷻ has made him aware of.

He doesn't say لَن تَصْبِرَ (you won't be patient) but rather لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ (you won't be able). This is stronger—it's about capability, not just choice. He's saying, "This isn't within your capacity. You can't handle this. It's beyond you."

translation: And how can you have patience for what you do not encompass in knowledge
commentary:
وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلَىٰ مَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ خُبْرًا

He explains why: وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ (And how can you have patience) عَلَىٰ مَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ خُبْرًا (over something which you have not encompassed in knowledge)?

The word تُحِطْ means to encircle or encompass completely. خُبْرًا refers to full news, complete information. He's saying, "How can you be patient when you see something that doesn't make sense to you, something you can't wrap your head around, something for which you don't have the full picture?"

This reveals something about Musa's (AS) nature and his track record. When he sees something that doesn't make sense, it upsets him, and he acts to rectify it. Remember when he was in Madyan? He saw two young women struggling, standing back while a crowd of shepherds watered their flocks. These girls were wrestling with their sheep—the animals pulling so hard the girls were dragged along, then pushing back in a tug of war. Musa (AS) looked at this scene and thought, "This doesn't make sense." So he approached them and asked, مَا خَطْبُكُمَا (What is your circumstance? What's going on?). What he really meant was: Why are you girls doing this work? Don't you have a brother, a father, a husband—someone else who should be helping you? Why aren't you getting access to the water?

This is Musa's (AS) characteristic: when he encounters something that seems wrong or illogical, and there's more to the story than he can see, he intervenes. He seeks to resolve things he can't fully understand. The teacher is essentially telling him, "You have a certain tendency—a good tendency, the tendency of a prophet to establish justice—but that tendency won't work with this particular program. The prerequisites aren't going to be met."

translation: [Moses] said, "You will find me, if Allah wills, patient, and I will not disobey you in [any] order
commentary:
قَالَ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ صَابِرًا وَلَآ أَعْصِى لَكَ أَمْرًا

Musa (AS) responds: سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ صَابِرًا (You will find me, if Allah wills, patient).

This is a crucial moment in the surah, a case study in saying إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ. We learn from this passage that whenever you speak about yourself and the future, you should add insha'Allah. Musa (AS) says, "You'll find me patient, insha'Allah."

But then notice what comes next: وَلَآ أَعْصِى لَكَ أَمْرًا (And I will not disobey you in any matter). Wait—what's missing here? He didn't add insha'Allah to this second part. He said insha'Allah about being patient, but about not disobeying? He stated that absolutely.

This is the case study of insha'Allah embedded in this narrative. Musa (AS) made two promises: "I will be patient, insha'Allah" and "I will not disobey you at all." As we'll see, he did ask questions, but he remained patient between incidents. However, the absolute statement—"I will not disobey you"—that's the one that didn't quite work out as planned.

translation: He said, "Then if you follow me, do not ask me about anything until I make to you about it mention
commentary:
قَالَ فَإِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَنِى فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِى عَن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْرًا

The teacher lays down the ground rules: فَإِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَنِى (If you follow me) فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِى عَن شَىْءٍ (then do not ask me about anything).

He doesn't just say "don't ask me" and stop there. He emphasizes: عَن شَىْءٍ (about anything)—not anything at all. Don't even think about asking me a question unrelated to what we're doing. No questions about the class, no questions unrelated to the class—no questions period.

حَتَّىٰٓ أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْرًا (Until I myself mention to you something about it, until I bring up the explanation). The word أُحْدِثَ means to bring something new into being, to initiate. ذِكْرًا here means explanation or clarification. "Don't ask me anything until I decide to explain it to you myself. That's my condition. That's the student code of conduct in my class."

translation: So they set out, until when they had embarked on the ship, al-Khidh r tore it open. [Moses] said, "Have you torn it open to drown its people? You have certainly done a grave thing
commentary:
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِى ٱلسَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَهَا ۖ قَالَ أَخَرَقْتَهَا لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَهَا لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا إِمْرًا

فَٱنطَلَقَا (So they set out) حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِى ٱلسَّفِينَةِ (until when they had boarded a ship). They're traveling together now, and they board a vessel.

خَرَقَهَا—he tore it, he made a hole in it. The word خَرَق means to tear or rip. Imagine the scene: they're on the ship, and the teacher takes Musa (AS) down to the lower deck and tears off a plank. Water starts gushing in.

Musa (AS) immediately reacts: أَخَرَقْتَهَا لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَهَا (Have you torn it to drown its people?). His concern is immediate and practical—we're on this boat, and you just made a hole that's going to sink it and drown everyone aboard, including us.

لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا إِمْرًا (You have certainly done something imra). The word إِمْر is remarkable—it captures something that is both unethical and illogical at the same time. It's morally wrong and it makes no practical sense. Why would you destroy the very vessel we're traveling on? This is both evil and absurd.

translation: [Al-Khidh r] said, "Did I not say that with me you would never be able to have patience
commentary:
قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا

The teacher's response is simple and direct: أَلَمْ أَقُلْ (Didn't I say?) إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (that you would never be able to have patience with me?).

He quotes his own words back exactly. "These were my exact words—that you are not capable of patience with me. Not with other people perhaps, but specifically not with me."

translation: [Moses] said, "Do not blame me for what I forgot and do not cover me in my matter with difficulty
commentary:
قَالَ لَا تُؤَاخِذْنِى بِمَا نَسِيتُ وَلَا تُرْهِقْنِى مِنْ أَمْرِى عُسْرًا

Musa (AS) immediately apologizes. قَالَ لَا تُؤَاخِذْنِى بِمَا نَسِيتُ (He said: Do not blame me for what I forgot).

The word تُؤَاخِذْ means to hold someone accountable, to seize them for their error, to rebuke or scold them. "Don't punish me as a teacher would punish a student. Don't hold me responsible." 

بِمَا نَسِيتُ (For what I forgot). Now this is deeply ironic and beautiful. Who forgot something earlier in this story? The young assistant forgot to mention the fish, and he said فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ (I forgot the fish). Now here is Musa (AS) using the exact same word: نَسِيتُ (I forgot). 

But notice something remarkable about Musa's (AS) character and maturity. The young man, when he forgot, immediately said وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ (Nothing made me forget except Shaytan)—he deflected blame. But Musa (AS) doesn't say "Shaytan made me forget." He doesn't deflect. He simply owns it: "I forgot." He takes full responsibility. This shows the maturity and integrity of a prophet.

وَلَا تُرْهِقْنِى مِنْ أَمْرِى عُسْرًا (And do not impose difficulty upon me in my affair). The word تُرْهِقْ means to overwhelm, to burden, to impose a heavy weight upon someone. There's an Arabic expression using this word that means to crush someone with harshness. 

عُسْرًا (Difficulty, hardship). He's saying, "Take it easy on me. Don't be too harsh. Don't crush me with your severity. I made a mistake—don't make this harder than it needs to be. Please, just ease up a little."

In essence: "Okay, okay, calm down. I'm sorry. Don't be so hard on me about this decision I made, this reaction I had. Please show me some leniency."

translation: So they set out, until when they met a boy, al-Khidh r killed him. [Moses] said, "Have you killed a pure soul for other than [having killed] a soul? You have certainly done a deplorable thing
commentary:
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا لَقِيَا غُلَـٰمًا فَقَتَلَهُۥ قَالَ أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا زَكِيَّةًۢ بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَّقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا نُّكْرًا

They continued on their journey until they encountered a young boy, perhaps eight or ten years old. Without warning, Al-Khidr killed him. Musa (AS), shocked and distressed, immediately questioned this extreme action: "Have you killed an innocent soul without him having killed anyone?"

The word زَكِيَّةً (zakiyyah) describes this boy as pure and innocent. The root also carries the meaning of intelligence—zakī refers to someone who is both good and bright. This makes the act even more incomprehensible from Musa's (AS) perspective: this wasn't just any child, but a pure, intelligent young person with apparent potential.

Musa's (AS) objection intensifies: بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ—without any soul, meaning the boy hadn't murdered anyone to warrant such punishment. This wasn't retaliation or justice in any recognizable form.

Then comes his second criticism: لَّقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا نُّكْرًا—"You have done something nukra." While the first incident was described as imra (strange, troubling), this second incident is nukra—something utterly alien to human conscience, unrecognizable as acceptable in any society. Nukra is something so foreign to basic morality that it cannot be rationalized or accepted anywhere. The murder of a child, especially an innocent one, is universally condemned. No civilization would accept this. It's the appropriate word for an act that shocks the very core of human decency.

translation: [Al-Khidh r] said, "Did I not tell you that with me you would never be able to have patience
commentary:
۞ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا

Al-Khidr's response reveals his growing frustration. Notice the subtle but significant change in his words: أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكَ—"Didn't I tell *you*?" The word لَّكَ (to you) is now explicitly stated, whereas before he had simply said, "You will not be able to have patience with me." The addition shows his rising exasperation.

He's getting upset, and when people become more emotional, they tend to say more. This is a beautiful touch in the Quranic narrative—the dialogue reflects genuine human emotion and interaction, yet maintains its dignity and purpose.

translation: [Moses] said, "If I should ask you about anything after this, then do not keep me as a companion. You have obtained from me an excuse
commentary:
قَالَ إِن سَأَلْتُكَ عَن شَىْءٍۭ بَعْدَهَا فَلَا تُصَـٰحِبْنِى ۖ قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِن لَّدُنِّى عُذْرًا

Musa (AS) realizes he's on thin ice. He makes what amounts to a final plea: "If I ask you about anything after this, then do not keep me as a companion. You will have reached, as far as I'm concerned, a sufficient excuse." 

He's essentially saying: "One more transgression on my part, and I will not complain if you expel me from your company. You'll be completely justified in leaving me behind." This is Musa's (AS) last chance, and he knows it. The condition is now explicit and final.

translation: So they set out, until when they came to the people of a town, they asked its people for food, but they refused to offer them hospitality. And they found therein a wall about to collapse, so al-Khidh r restored it. [Moses] said, "If you wished, you could have taken for it a payment
commentary:
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَيَآ أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ ٱسْتَطْعَمَآ أَهْلَهَا فَأَبَوْا۟ أَن يُضَيِّفُوهُمَا فَوَجَدَا فِيهَا جِدَارًا يُرِيدُ أَن يَنقَضَّ فَأَقَامَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا

They continued until they reached أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ—the people of a town. The wording is significant: Allah ﷻ doesn't say they reached a town, but rather they reached its people. This suggests they encountered the townspeople before even fully entering, and quickly learned what kind of people they were dealing with.

After a long journey, the two travelers asked for food—ٱسْتَطْعَمَآ means to request food, a reasonable need for weary travelers. Musa (AS) had experience with long journeys; he had traveled before and even found employment after one such journey. But this town offered no such hospitality.

فَأَبَوْا۟ أَن يُضَيِّفُوهُمَا—they refused to host them. Doors were shut in their faces. "We don't have food here. Try the next town." This complete lack of hospitality tells us much about the character of these people. Already we can see what kind of community this is—one lacking in basic human decency and generosity.

Walking through the town, they found a wall—جِدَارًا يُرِيدُ أَن يَنقَضَّ—a wall that "wanted" to collapse. The verb يُرِيدُ (to want) is remarkable here. How can a wall want anything? This is a vivid expression in Arabic: when a structure is leaning, cracked, about to give way, you say it "wants to fall." It's bent on falling, ready to collapse at any moment.

Without being asked and without any promise of payment, Al-Khidr repaired the wall—فَأَقَامَهُۥ—he set it straight, fixed it properly.

Musa (AS), mindful of his promise not to object, phrases his observation carefully: لَوْ شِئْتَ لَتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا—"If you had wanted, you could have taken compensation for it." Notice he doesn't ask "Why didn't you ask for payment?" That would be a question, another objection. He's trying to be clever, staying within the letter of his agreement while still expressing his bewilderment. The statement is observational rather than interrogative.

But the teacher sees right through it. Sometimes students try to get around the rules with technicalities, and wise teachers recognize it immediately.

translation: [Al-Khidh r] said, "This is parting between me and you. I will inform you of the interpretation of that about which you could not have patience
commentary:
قَالَ هَـٰذَا فِرَاقُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ ۚ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ مَا لَمْ تَسْتَطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا

Al-Khidr's response is decisive: هَـٰذَا فِرَاقُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ—"This is the parting between me and you." Notice the word order: بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ—between me and you. He mentions himself first. "I'm leaving. You can stay here if you want, but I'm done. This journey ends here."

But he doesn't leave Musa (AS) in complete bewilderment. سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ—"I will inform you of the interpretation"—the inner reality, the true meaning behind these apparently inexplicable actions. Before parting ways, he will explain what you couldn't bear to remain patient over.

translation: As for the ship, it belonged to poor people working at sea. So I intended to cause defect in it as there was after them a king who seized every [good] ship by force
commentary:
أَمَّا ٱلسَّفِينَةُ فَكَانَتْ لِمَسَـٰكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَعِيبَهَا وَكَانَ وَرَآءَهُم مَّلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْبًا

Now the explanations begin, each starting with أَمَّا (as for), addressing the three incidents in order.

"As for the ship, it belonged to poor people who worked at sea." They were either fishermen or perhaps operated a ferry service, making their living from this vessel. It was their livelihood, their only means of survival.

فَأَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَعِيبَهَا—"So I intended to damage it." Notice carefully: he says "I intended." The verb used is أَرَدتُّ (I wanted), and أَعِيبَهَا means to cause a flaw, to create a defect. He's taking ownership of the action at this point in the explanation.

The reason becomes clear: وَكَانَ وَرَآءَهُم مَّلِكٌ—"And there was behind them a king." The word وَرَآءَ means beyond their view, just around the corner, though they couldn't see him. Picture waterways with turns and bends—the king's naval forces were right there, just out of sight. 

يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْبًا—he was seizing every ship by force. Ghașban means oppressive confiscation, grabbing what isn't yours, commandeering vessels to build his navy or for whatever purpose he desired. Any intact, functional ship that came into his waters, he took.

But a damaged ship? One that appeared to be half-sinking, barely functional? That one he might spare. The poor owners could keep their damaged vessel, repair it later, and preserve their livelihood.

translation: And as for the boy, his parents were believers, and we feared that he would overburden them by transgression and disbelief
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلْغُلَـٰمُ فَكَانَ أَبَوَاهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينَآ أَن يُرْهِقَهُمَا طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا

"As for the boy, both his parents were believers." This detail is crucial—his mother and father were both people of faith, righteous individuals.

Yet فَخَشِينَآ—"we feared." Notice the shift to "we," which will become significant. What did they fear? أَن يُرْهِقَهُمَا طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا—that he would compel them, force them into rebellion and disbelief.

Remember that earlier this boy was described as zakī—pure and intelligent. Intelligence can lead to tremendous good or terrible evil. An evil genius is still a genius. This boy, had he lived, would have been so compelling, so powerful in personality and intellect, that he wouldn't merely rebel himself—he would force his influence upon his parents. His tugyan (rebellion) and kufr (disbelief) would be so strong that he'd drag his believing parents down with him, overwhelming them until they too fell into disbelief.

Another possible reading is that the boy himself would be forced into these states, that he would oppress himself with rebellion and disbelief. Either interpretation reveals a tragic future averted.

translation: So we intended that their Lord should substitute for them one better than him in purity and nearer to mercy
commentary:
فَأَرَدْنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَوٰةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا

Here comes another crucial shift: فَأَرَدْنَآ—"So we intended." Previously it was "I intended" regarding the ship. Now it's "we intended," uniting himself with Allah's ﷻ will in this matter.

أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا—"that their Master should replace for them." Notice: "their Master," their Rabb, will provide the replacement. خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَوٰةً—someone better than him in zakah, in that same quality of purity and intelligence that the first boy possessed, but directed toward good.

وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا—and closer in ruhm. This word combines both mercy and family ties. The replacement wouldn't just be a better person morally, but would maintain closer family bonds, be more dutiful, more loving. He would be a better son in every way—smarter, purer, and more devoted to his parents and family.

translation: And as for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was beneath it a treasure for them, and their father had been righteous. So your Lord intended that they reach maturity and extract their treasure, as a mercy from your Lord. And I did it not of my own accord. That is the interpretation of that about which you could not have patience
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَـٰمَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُۥ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ أَن يَبْلُغَآ أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا فَعَلْتُهُۥ عَنْ أَمْرِى ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ مَا لَمْ تَسْطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا

"As for the wall, it belonged to two young boys, orphans in the city." We already know what kind of people inhabit this city—the ones who refused basic hospitality to hungry travelers. Imagine how they would treat vulnerable orphans. The situation is precarious.

وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُۥ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا—beneath the wall was a treasure belonging to them. It was theirs, their inheritance, but they couldn't access it yet. The treasure and the orphans are mentioned together because they belong together, yet circumstances kept them apart.

Then comes a powerful addition: وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا—"and their father had been righteous." He was a good man, a man of faith and character. This detail isn't accidental. Allah ﷻ could have simply mentioned the orphans and their treasure, but He adds that their father was righteous. Why?

Because Allah ﷻ was now protecting and providing for the children of a righteous man, even after his death. The father's righteousness brought barakah (blessing) that extended beyond his lifetime, safeguarding his children when he could no longer do so himself. This is the legacy of the righteous—Allah ﷻ watches over what they leave behind.

فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ—"So your Master intended." Again, when speaking of the clearly good outcome, the action is attributed directly to Allah ﷻ. أَن يَبْلُغَآ أَشُدَّهُمَا—that both of them should reach their mature age, their full strength. وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا—and then extract their treasure, when they're strong enough to protect it.

The treasure was theirs, but premature access would have been disastrous. The greedy townspeople would have found ways to snatch it from vulnerable orphans. They needed to grow, to mature, to reach a point of strength where they could defend their inheritance. رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ—all of this was mercy from your Master.

Then comes the final, essential clarification: وَمَا فَعَلْتُهُۥ عَنْ أَمْرِى—"And I did not do any of this from my own command." Everything Musa (AS) witnessed, everything that seemed inexplicable or wrong—none of it was Al-Khidr acting on his own authority or judgment. It was all divine instruction.

ذَٰلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ مَا لَمْ تَسْطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا—"That is the interpretation of what you could not bear with patience." Notice even here, in his final words, there's a subtle terseness. He doesn't say تَسْتَطِع (the full form), but تَسْطِع (the shortened form)—as if he's in such a hurry to depart that he's cutting his words short. The relationship has reached its end.

---

## Deeper Reflections

The three stops in this journey reveal profound wisdom about Allah's ﷻ planning and our limited understanding of His ways.

The first incident was economic—saving poor people from financial ruin. The second was social and familial—protecting a family from corruption and society from a future criminal. The third was about social welfare—preserving the inheritance of vulnerable orphans. In each case, there was immediate, apparent harm followed by long-term benefit.

This teaches us that Allah's ﷻ plans operate on timelines we cannot see. It's not merely dunya versus akhirah—it's also dunya now versus dunya twenty years from now. Sometimes we must take one step back to take twenty steps forward. What appears harmful today may be protective tomorrow. What seems like loss may be preservation.

The story also teaches us about appropriate speech regarding divine decree. Notice the careful distribution of pronouns throughout the explanations: "I intended" for the apparently harmful act of damaging the ship. "We intended" for the complex situation involving both the killing and the replacement. "Your Master intended" for the clearly beneficial act of preserving the orphans' inheritance.

There's an outer reality and an inner reality to every event. The outer reality may appear bad, but the inner reality—which Allah ﷻ knows—is good. Most people aren't mature enough to judge by inner reality; they judge only by what they can see externally. This leads them to inappropriate conclusions and even statements about Allah ﷻ.

When the ship was damaged—outwardly bad—Al-Khidr took responsibility in his speech. When the wall was built—outwardly good—he attributed it to Allah ﷻ. The middle incident involved both harm (the killing) and benefit (the replacement), so he used "we," combining himself with divine will, but then immediately shifted to "your Master" when mentioning the specific benefit of the replacement.

He doesn't even repeat the full details of what occurred. He simply says "As for the ship... as for the boy... as for the wall," giving each a brief title before explaining. This is deliberate restraint, especially regarding the most difficult incident to discuss.

Finally, we must address the objection some raise about this narrative. Critics claim the Quran condones killing children, but this misunderstands both the nature of prophethood and divine command. This was a prophet receiving specific divine instruction for a unique situation—just as Ibrahim (AS) was commanded regarding Ismail (AS), something even critics' own traditions acknowledge.

Prophets are given extraordinarily difficult tests that ordinary believers never face. No legitimate Islamic interpretation has ever suggested this incident sets a precedent for general behavior. When Allah ﷻ describes those who mock revelation, He says they take it as a joke—وَاتَّخَذُوا آيَاتِنَا هُزُوًا. That's what such criticism amounts to: mockery disguised as moral concern.

The appropriate response is to remember what Allah ﷻ told His Messenger ﷺ: وَاتْلُ مَا أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِن كِتَابِ رَبِّكَ—"Recite what has been revealed to you from your Master's Book. No one will change these words." Don't worry about those who refuse to understand. وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا—"And do not obey from among them any sinner or denier." Ignore that noise. Continue with the message you've been given.

The story of Musa (AS) and Al-Khidr reminds us that our knowledge is limited, that divine wisdom operates beyond our immediate comprehension, and that trust in Allah ﷻ sometimes means accepting that we don't have all the answers. The inner realities will be revealed in time—if not in this life, then certainly in the next.

translation: And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about Dhul-Qarnayn. Say, "I will recite to you about him a report
commentary:
وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَن ذِى ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ ۖ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا

We arrive at the final story in Surah Al-Kahf - the account of Dhul-Qarnayn. This narrative completes a remarkable sequence: the people of the cave, the two gardeners, the journeys of Musa (AS) and his teacher, and now this righteous ruler whose story the Jews themselves asked about.

The Jews of Madinah posed this question to the Prophet ﷺ as a test, asking about a figure celebrated in their own historical tradition. But who was Dhul-Qarnayn? In early Islamic scholarship, access to global historical sources was limited, leading to various interpretations. Some even suggested he might be Alexander the Great, given the expansive nature of his kingdom spanning from east to west.

As Islamic civilization expanded and historians gained access to broader historical records, a more substantiated view emerged. The most widely accepted scholarly opinion today identifies Dhul-Qarnayn as Cyrus the Great of Persia, known in Persian as Kourosh. Archaeological evidence supports this identification remarkably. When Iran celebrated the 2,500th anniversary of his reign approximately forty years ago, excavations uncovered monuments depicting his crown adorned with two horns - directly corresponding to his Quranic title, which literally means "the one with two horns."

The historical context explains this symbolism beautifully. Persia had been divided into two separate kingdoms, and Cyrus unified them before expanding the Persian Empire further. By uniting these two kingdoms, he symbolically wore a horn on his crown representing each realm - a powerful image of bringing together what had been separated.

But why would the Jewish community ask about a Persian ruler? The connection runs deep through their history. Around 587 BCE, Nebuchadnezzar conquered Jerusalem and took hundreds of thousands of Jewish people as slaves to Babylon, where they remained in captivity for approximately 150 years. When the Persians conquered Babylon - most historians argue under Cyrus's rule - the Jewish people were granted permission to leave and return to Jerusalem. This liberation made him a celebrated figure in Jewish tradition, someone who showed them great favor and mercy.

This history unfolded roughly four to five hundred years before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ received revelation, making Dhul-Qarnayn a significant historical figure whose legacy persisted through the centuries.

What we can piece together from historical sources suggests a remarkable upbringing. Though born into royalty, an assassination attempt forced him into hiding in the remote regions of Persia. During this time in obscurity, he apparently encountered either a prophet or prophetic teachings, and was raised with Islamic monotheism and values. When he eventually assumed power, he applied these divinely-inspired principles to his governance.

The Quran hints at his relationship with divine guidance when it mentions that Allah ﷻ spoke to him, saying "either punish them or treat them with kindness." Scholars differ on the nature of this communication. The most conservative view suggests he received ilham - divine inspiration that guided his feelings and decisions. The other perspective considers the possibility that he was himself a prophet, since Allah ﷻ spoke directly to him. This remains a matter of scholarly discussion.

When Allah ﷻ says "they ask you about Dhul-Qarnayn," He instructs the Prophet ﷺ: "Say, I will recite to you some mention of him." Notice the beautiful precision - not everything about him, just some things. This is why historians continue researching and attempting to fill in the gaps, because only portions of his story have been revealed to us.

The grammatical structure here deserves attention. The verb سَأَتْلُوا۟ (I will recite) uses the future tense without the typical conditional "if Allah wills," which seems to contradict the lesson learned earlier in this very surah about always saying insha'Allah. However, this apparent contradiction dissolves when we recognize that the command itself comes from Allah ﷻ - when He tells you to say something about the future, that itself becomes the exception to the rule.

translation: Indeed We established him upon the earth, and We gave him to everything a way
commentary:
إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ سَبَبًا

The foundation of Dhul-Qarnayn's accomplishments begins with Allah's favor. "We had certainly established him in the land" - the word مَكَّنَّا conveys the idea of granting stability, authority and firm grounding. This wasn't just political power, but comprehensive establishment that enabled effective governance.

Then comes a remarkable phrase: "and We gave him to everything a means." The beauty of this statement lies in what remains unsaid. The object of what Allah ﷻ gave him is deliberately omitted, leaving it open and comprehensive. This grammatical choice suggests He furnished Dhul-Qarnayn with all types of resources - military strength, financial wealth, social structures, material assets and natural resources. Everything necessary to accomplish his mission was placed at his disposal.

The word سَبَبًا itself carries multiple layers of meaning. Its primary sense is "means" or "resources" - the tools and utilities to accomplish tasks. But sabab also means "a path" or "a mission." This wordplay becomes significant in the next ayah, as we see Dhul-Qarnayn didn't merely possess resources - he used them purposefully, pursuing missions that would benefit humanity.

translation: So he followed a way
commentary:
فَأَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا

"So he followed a way" - this simple statement marks the beginning of three great journeys. Having been granted the means, Dhul-Qarnayn now pursues a mission. Historical evidence suggests these journeys took him westward toward the Mediterranean coast, then eastward all the way to what is now the Makran coast of Pakistan, and finally to the mountainous regions between the Caspian and Black Seas.

The verb أَتْبَعَ indicates following through, pursuing a course of action with determination. He didn't simply possess power and resources - he actively used them to explore, govern justly and address the needs of various peoples. This becomes the pattern repeated throughout his story: he follows a way, encounters a people, and applies divine guidance in dealing with them.

translation: Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it [as if] setting in a spring of dark mud, and he found near it a people. Allah said, "O Dhul-Qarnayn, either you punish [them] or else adopt among them [a way of] goodness
commentary:
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِى عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِندَهَا قَوْمًا ۗ قُلْنَا يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّآ أَن تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّآ أَن تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا

His first journey took him to مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ - "the setting place of the sun." This is clearly descriptive imagery, not a literal claim about cosmology. He reached the western coast and witnessed the sun setting over the horizon. The Quran describes what he observed: "he found it setting in a spring of dark mud."

Some critics have attempted to claim this represents a scientific error in the Quran, as though it teaches the sun literally drowns in water. This misses the obvious literary nature of the description. Such phrasing appears constantly in human language across all cultures - we speak of sunrise and sunset, of the sun "going down," even though we understand the Earth's rotation. The Quran describes his visual perception from the shoreline - the sun appearing to sink into the murky waters of the sea.

The description عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ (spring of dark mud) uses the word حَمِئَة to convey murky, thick water - not the clear, pristine water of a spring, but the muddy appearance of coastal waters at sunset.

Near this location, he encountered a people. Having conquered this nation through his military expedition, Dhul-Qarnayn faced the universal question of power: what will you do with those you've defeated? 

Here Allah ﷻ presents him with a choice - whether through direct revelation or divine inspiration: "O Dhul-Qarnayn, either you punish them or you treat them with kindness." This wasn't permission for tyranny, but rather a test of his character and judgment. How would he rule? Would he abuse his authority or apply it justly?

The choice itself reveals profound wisdom about governance. Justice sometimes requires punishment for wrongdoing, while mercy and good treatment benefit those who deserve it. A just ruler must have the wisdom to distinguish between these situations and the character to apply each appropriately.

translation: He said, "As for one who wrongs, we will punish him. Then he will be returned to his Lord, and He will punish him with a terrible punishment
commentary:
قَالَ أَمَّا مَن ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُۥ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ فَيُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا

Dhul-Qarnayn's response reveals the depth of his faith and the sophistication of his governance. "As for one who wrongs, we will punish him." He establishes immediately that his kingdom operates on justice - criminal behavior will face consequences. This isn't arbitrary cruelty but the rule of law.

But notice what he adds: "Then he will be returned to his Lord, and He will punish him with a terrible punishment." This statement accomplishes multiple objectives. First, it reminds both the wrongdoer and the public that earthly punishment isn't the end - accountability continues in the Hereafter. Second, it demonstrates that Dhul-Qarnayn's authority derives from and remains subservient to divine authority. He punishes as a ruler enforcing justice, but ultimate judgment belongs to Allah ﷻ.

The phrase عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (a terrible punishment) uses a word meaning something unprecedented, unheard of, unimaginable. Whatever punishment the criminal faces in this world pales in comparison to what awaits in the next. This serves as both warning and deterrent - a public declaration establishing law and order in his newly governed territory.

translation: But as for one who believes and does righteousness, he will have a reward of Paradise, and we will speak to him from our command with ease
commentary:
وَأَمَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُۥ جَزَآءً ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا

Having established consequences for wrongdoing, Dhul-Qarnayn immediately balances this with the promise of reward for righteousness. "But as for one who believes and does righteousness, he will have a reward of the best."

The grammatical structure here contains beautiful subtlety. The phrase فَلَهُۥ جَزَآءً ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ literally says "for him is a reward - the very best one." The word ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ functions as what's called a bayān (clarification) in Arabic grammar. He doesn't just promise compensation - he specifies the very best compensation possible.

Then he adds a practical dimension to this spiritual promise: "and we will speak to him from our command with ease." Even in the administration of governance - collecting taxes, issuing directives, civic engagement - those who do good will be treated with kindness, civility and ease. There's a softness in how the righteous are addressed, a gentleness in how authority interacts with them.

This balanced approach to governance - strict with criminals, generous with the righteous - establishes both justice and mercy. It creates a society where people feel both the weight of accountability and the warmth of good treatment, depending on their choices.

translation: Then he followed a way
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا

Having established justice in the western territory, Dhul-Qarnayn doesn't settle into comfortable rule. "Then he followed a way" - the phrase repeats, indicating his continuing mission. Leadership isn't about accumulating power but about fulfilling responsibility across all domains under one's care.

This repetition of the same phrase creates a rhythmic pattern in the narrative, emphasizing that his approach remains consistent regardless of location or circumstance. He moves from one mission to the next, always guided by the same principles.

translation: Until, when he came to the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had not made against it any shield
commentary:
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَطْلُعُ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ لَّمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُم مِّن دُونِهَا سِتْرًا

His second journey took him eastward to مَطْلِعَ ٱلشَّمْسِ - "the rising place of the sun." If the first journey headed west toward the sunset, this one heads east toward the sunrise. Again, this is descriptive language indicating direction, not a cosmological claim.

What he discovered there reveals something fascinating about human civilization's diversity: "He found it rising on a people for whom We had not made against it any shield." These people lived in such architecturally primitive conditions that they didn't even have roofs over their houses. The word سِتْرًا means covering or barrier - they had no protection from the sun's direct rays.

This wasn't necessarily a judgment against them, but simply an observation about their stage of technological development. They lived in dwellings separated perhaps by walls, but open to the sky. The detail matters because it shows Dhul-Qarnayn encountered peoples at vastly different levels of civilization, yet he applied the same just principles to all of them.

translation: Thus. And We had encompassed [all] that he had in knowledge
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنَا بِمَا لَدَيْهِ خُبْرًا

This brief ayah contains a profound statement: "Thus. And We had encompassed all that he had in knowledge."

The word كَذَٰلِكَ ("thus" or "just like that") captures an entire interaction in one phrase. What does it refer to? It indicates that Dhul-Qarnayn's response to this eastern nation was exactly the same as his response to the western nation. The same choice was presented to him - punish or show kindness - and he responded with the same balanced approach: justice for wrongdoers, generosity for the righteous.

Rather than narrating the entire conversation again, Allah ﷻ simply says "thus" - meaning it happened just as before. This economy of language emphasizes the consistency of just governance. A righteous ruler doesn't change his principles based on which people he's governing.

Then comes the powerful reminder: "And We had encompassed all that he had in knowledge." Allah ﷻ knew completely what transpired with him, how he dealt with that nation, what choices he made, what justice he established. The Quran gives us glimpses and pieces, but Allah's knowledge encompasses the full reality. This serves as both validation of Dhul-Qarnayn's righteousness and a reminder that all leaders ultimately answer to the One whose knowledge encompasses everything.

translation: Then he followed a way
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا

Once again, "Then he followed a way." The repetition now takes on even greater significance - this marks the third journey, the third mission. Having established justice in the west and the east, he now heads to yet another region requiring his attention and leadership.

translation: Until, when he reached [a pass] between two mountains, he found beside them a people who could hardly understand [his] speech
commentary:
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمَا قَوْمًا لَّا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا

This third journey takes him to a mountainous region - بَيْنَ ٱلسَّدَّيْنِ means "between the two barriers" or "between the two mountains." Based on historical analysis, this refers to the mountain range between the Caspian and Black Seas. The word سَدّ specifically refers to a mountain when it relates to water passage, which is why the same word later came to mean "dam."

There he encountered perhaps the most unusual people yet: "He found before them a people who could barely understand any speech." The phrase لَّا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا uses yakaduna (almost) - indicating they almost don't understand anything. Their language was so obscure, so different from anything he'd encountered, that communication seemed nearly impossible.

This creates an immediate practical challenge. How do you govern justly when you cannot even communicate with the people? How do you establish law and order when basic conversation fails?

The beauty of يَكَادُونَ (almost) suggests that despite this immense difficulty, communication wasn't completely impossible. Perhaps through gestures, sign language, persistent effort and mutual desire to understand, they found ways to bridge the gap. This determination to communicate despite barriers speaks to the character of both Dhul-Qarnayn and these people.

translation: They said, "O Dhul-Qarnayn, indeed Gog and Magog are [great] corrupters in the land. So may we assign for you an expenditure that you might make between us and them a barrier
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجًا عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا

Having somehow managed to communicate, these people immediately recognized Dhul-Qarnayn's capacity for justice and protection. Rather than begging for mercy for themselves, they brought him their problem: "O Dhul-Qarnayn, indeed Yajuj and Majuj are corrupters in the land."

This introduces us to Yajuj and Majuj (Gog and Magog) - a people mentioned twice in the Quran, and the subject of much eschatological discussion. What we learn here is that they were a warrior nation who would periodically raid through the mountain passes, pillaging villages, taking resources and causing widespread destruction before retreating.

The people's proposal shows both their desperation and their respect: "So may we assign for you an expenditure that you might make between us and them a barrier?" The word خَرْجًا literally means "extraction" - they're offering to extract from their own budget, to set aside financial resources to fund this protective project.

The image becomes vivid when you understand the geography. These raiders would come through where the river passes through the mountains, sweep down upon the villages, take what they wanted and leave. The people needed a barrier that would prevent this passage entirely.

What requires serious historical and archaeological research is the identity and ultimate fate of Yajuj and Majuj. Some historical sources, including interpretations of Biblical genealogy, suggest they were among the descendants of Yafith (Japheth), and that various Eastern and Western European nations trace their lineage to him. Within Islamic tradition, some have speculatively identified them with Eastern peoples, leading to theories about the Great Wall of China - but this lacks solid evidence.

What we do know is that the dam Dhul-Qarnayn built has actually been located in that mountain ridge, and Muslim historians should continue conducting archaeological research and documentation of these sites. This isn't merely academic curiosity - it's about understanding our revealed text through the evidence Allah ﷻ has left in His creation.

translation: He said, "That in which my Lord has established me is better [than what you offer], but assist me with strength; I will make between you and them a dam
commentary:
قَالَ مَا مَكَّنِّى فِيهِ رَبِّى خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِى بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْمًا

Dhul-Qarnayn's response reveals remarkable character: "That in which my Lord has established me is better." He refuses their money. Despite having the authority to accept tribute from conquered peoples, despite the justification that this project would benefit them, he declines their financial offering.

Why? Because Allah ﷻ had already provided him with everything he needed. Remember the earlier ayah: "We gave him to everything a means." He had military resources, financial resources, material resources. He didn't need their wealth, and taking it would have created an unequal power dynamic - as though they were purchasing his protection.

But notice what he does request: "So assist me with strength." He doesn't want their money, but he does want their manpower, their physical participation. This reflects profound wisdom about leadership and community building. He could have simply built the dam for them using his own resources and moved on. Instead, he says, "This is your nation, your land, your problem. I will help you, but you must be involved too."

This is the difference between creating dependency and empowering people. By requiring their participation, he teaches them construction techniques, problem-solving and self-reliance. They will understand how the dam was built and potentially maintain or repair it themselves.

Then he makes a critical distinction in terminology: "I will make between you and them a radm." Notice they asked for a سَدّ (a dam), but he promises a رَدْم (a rampart). What's the difference?

A regular sadd might be a single wall or barrier, vulnerable to damage from earthquakes, erosion or enemy attack. A radm is far more sophisticated - it consists of two thick walls with sand packed between them. This creates flexibility that can withstand seismic activity, and depth that prevents penetration. The word radm comes from the root meaning to layer soil or sand, one layer upon another, filling a space until it becomes level with the surrounding ground.

He's not going to build their primitive barrier. He's going to construct a proper, engineered fortification that will truly protect them.

translation: Bring me sheets of iron" - until, when he had leveled [them] between the two mountain walls, he said, "Blow [with bellows]," until when he had made it [like] fire, he said, "Bring me, that I may pour over it molten copper
commentary:
ءَاتُونِى زُبَرَ ٱلْحَدِيدِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا سَاوَىٰ بَيْنَ ٱلصَّدَفَيْنِ قَالَ ٱنفُخُوا۟ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَعَلَهُۥ نَارًا قَالَ ءَاتُونِىٓ أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْرًا

Now begins the remarkable construction process, and Dhul-Qarnayn acts as instructor, teaching these people advanced engineering: "Bring me sheets of iron."

Large plates of iron would form the primary structure - the walls themselves made of metal. This already represents sophisticated metallurgy and construction knowledge far beyond what this primitive people possessed.

"Until when he had leveled between the two mountain cliffs" - the phrase ٱلصَّدَفَيْنِ refers to the two sides of the mountain passage, the cliffs on either side. He built up the iron walls until they reached from the base of the valley to the level of the mountain peaks on both sides, creating a complete barrier.

Then comes the refinement process. "He said, 'Blow!'" - directing them to pump air into furnaces, heating the iron. "Until when he had made it like fire" - the metal itself begins to glow red-hot, reaching temperatures where it becomes malleable or begins to melt.

At this critical point: "He said, 'Bring me molten copper to pour over it.'" This introduces the second element - قِطْر, molten copper. Why copper over iron? Copper provides a smooth, hard finish that serves multiple purposes.

First, the smooth surface of copper prevents scaling. Remember, Yajuj and Majuj needed to get over this barrier. Normally, you might throw a hooked rope that catches on rough surfaces, then climb up. But a completely smooth copper surface offers nothing for a hook to grip - it simply slides off.

Second, copper adds another layer of strength and protection to the iron core. The combination of iron and copper, with sand between the double walls, creates an incredibly resilient structure.

Third, the copper acts as a protective coating against weathering and corrosion, helping the dam endure through time.

This entire process - the iron walls, the packed earth or sand between them, the copper coating - represents engineering knowledge far beyond the capability of these people. Dhul-Qarnayn isn't just protecting them, he's educating them, showing them what's possible through proper application of knowledge and resources.

translation: So Gog and Magog were unable to pass over it, nor were they able [to effect] in it any penetration
commentary:
فَمَا ٱسْطَـٰعُوٓا۟ أَن يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ لَهُۥ نَقْبًا

The proof of the dam's effectiveness comes in this ayah: "So they were unable to scale it, nor were they able to bore through it."

Notice the Quran uses two different words for "able" here, both from the root طوع but in different forms - ٱسْطَـٰعُوا and ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا. This variation adds rhythmic beauty to the recitation while emphasizing two distinct types of inability.

They couldn't يَظْهَرُوهُ - scale it or climb over it. The smooth copper surface made it impossible to get a grip or throw a hook. No matter what techniques they tried, the barrier remained insurmountable from the outside.

They also couldn't نَقْبًا - bore through it or tunnel into it. The combination of copper, iron and the packed earth between the double walls created such density and strength that penetration became impossible with the tools and technology available to raiders.

This complete impermeability - neither over nor through - fulfilled the people's need for protection. The barrier worked exactly as intended, and Yajuj and Majuj's raids came to an end.

translation: [Dhul-Qarnayn] said, "This is a mercy from my Lord; but when the promise of my Lord comes, He will make it level, and ever is the promise of my Lord true
commentary:
قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى جَعَلَهُۥ دَكَّآءَ ۖ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى حَقًّا

At the moment of completion, when the dam stands as an engineering marvel and the people celebrate their newfound security, Dhul-Qarnayn makes a statement that reveals the essence of his faith: "This is a mercy from my Lord."

Not "This is my achievement." Not "Look at what I've built." Not "This is a monument to my engineering genius." He immediately attributes the success to Allah ﷻ - this is a mercy from my Master. The skill, the resources, the opportunity, the success - all of it came from divine favor, not personal prowess.

This demonstrates why Allah ﷻ chose to tell his story. Despite tremendous power, authority and accomplishment, Dhul-Qarnayn maintained complete humility before his Creator. The dam could have become a source of pride, a legacy to his own greatness. Instead, he uses it as a reminder of Allah's mercy.

But then he adds something that transforms this physical barrier into a spiritual lesson: "But when the promise of my Lord comes, He will make it level, and the promise of my Lord is ever true."

This statement serves multiple profound purposes. First, it reminds everyone - himself, the people he's helped, future generations - that all worldly constructions, no matter how impressive, are temporary. When Allah's appointed time comes, this impenetrable barrier will be leveled to dust.

Second, it points to the Hereafter, to accountability beyond this life. Some scholars understand "the promise of my Lord" as referring to the Day of Judgment itself, when all earthly achievements crumble and only deeds remain. Others interpret it as referring to the time when Yajuj and Majuj will, by Allah's permission, break through this barrier as one of the signs preceding the end times - an event mentioned in authentic hadith.

Third, and perhaps most significantly given the context, it serves as a rebuke to those who asked the question in the first place. Remember, the Jews of Madinah asked about Dhul-Qarnayn because they revered him in their tradition. Yet somewhere along the way, they had largely abandoned belief in the Hereafter - a fundamental pillar of faith had been lost.

So when Allah ﷻ reveals Dhul-Qarnayn's story, what does He emphasize? Not just his military conquests or engineering achievements, but his statement: "The promise of my Lord has always been true." The Hereafter is real. Accountability is certain. No matter what you build in this world, it will not endure into the next.

This transforms the entire narrative. The Jews asked about a historical hero expecting validation of worldly power and success. Instead, they received a reminder that even this great king they admired recognized the temporary nature of worldly accomplishments and the eternal reality of standing before Allah ﷻ.

translation: And We will leave them that day surging over each other, and [then] the Horn will be blown, and We will assemble them in [one] assembly
commentary:
وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِى بَعْضٍ ۖ وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ جَمْعًا

The surah concludes the story of Dhul-Qarnayn with a powerful eschatological vision: "And We will leave them that day surging over one another, and the Horn will be blown, and We will assemble them in assembly."

This ayah has been understood in multiple complementary ways, each adding depth to its meaning.

One interpretation focuses on Yajuj and Majuj themselves. Despite the impenetrable barrier holding them back now, a time will come - that day - when they will surge forth in waves upon waves. The word يَمُوجُ comes from the imagery of waves, like a river that keeps flowing and never runs out. They will pour out in such overwhelming numbers that it seems endless, wave upon wave of humanity emerging.

This connects to prophetic traditions describing their emergence as one of the major signs before the Day of Judgment - a population so vast that it staggers the imagination, coming in unstoppable waves across the earth.

Another layer of meaning takes "that day" to refer to the Day of Judgment itself. All of humanity will be left surging over one another - the masses of people from every generation, every nation, every era, all gathered together in numbers that boggle comprehension. The image of waves captures the overwhelming scale of humanity assembled for accountability.

The grammatical structure suggests a cause-and-effect relationship: "And the Horn will be blown, and We will assemble them in assembly" provides the reason for the surging. This is the second blowing of the horn - the first causes all living things to die, the second resurrects them for judgment. As people are raised and gathered, they surge together in incomprehensible multitudes.

Some scholars have beautifully connected both interpretations: until the Day of Judgment itself, you will not see an army, a population, a mass of humanity as overwhelming as Yajuj and Majuj emerging in waves. The only other time such staggering numbers will be witnessed is when the horn is blown that second time and all of humanity is gathered together - that's when you'll see something comparable to or exceeding those waves of people.

The phrase جَمْعًا at the end emphasizes totality - We will gather them completely, comprehensively, leaving none behind. Every single person who ever lived will be assembled.

This conclusion ties together the entire story. Dhul-Qarnayn built an impenetrable barrier that fulfilled its purpose in this world - protecting innocent people from raiders and corruption. But both he and we are reminded that all barriers eventually fall, all worldly constructions crumble, and all people - those who built barriers and those who were kept out, the powerful and the weak, the righteous and the corrupt - will stand together before Allah ﷻ.
