The story that began with a question about a historical king ends with a reminder about the King of all kings, before whom all history culminates and every soul finds its ultimate accountability.

translation: And We will present Hell that Day to the Disbelievers, on display
commentary:
وَعَرَضْنَا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَرْضًا

"And We will present Hell that Day to the disbelievers, on display."

Allah ﷻ declares that on the Day of Judgment, Hellfire will be presented to the disbelievers in a thorough, comprehensive manner. The word عَرْضًا emphasizes that it won't be hidden or distant—it will be right in front of them, impossible to ignore or deny. They will stare directly at what awaits them.

translation: Those whose eyes had been within a cover [removed] from My remembrance, and they were not able to hear
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ كَانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِى غِطَآءٍ عَن ذِكْرِى وَكَانُوا۟ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعًا

"Those whose eyes had been within a cover [removed] from My remembrance, and they were not able to hear."

This ayah describes those destined for this fate: people whose eyes were covered from Allah's remembrance. The word غِطَآءٍ means a cover or veil—something that blocks vision completely. Usually when discussing guidance and misguidance, the Quran mentions the heart (qalb), but here Allah ﷻ specifically mentions eyes. Why? Because throughout this surah, the focus has been on the visible signs—the signs of the Day of Judgment, the miraculous events described in the stories. These are things people will see, not just hear about. Yet even when confronted with visible proof, these people still won't change.

The ayah continues: "and they were not able to even listen." This phrase reveals a deeper problem. Before they could have seen these signs, they had already refused to listen to good advice. The revelation was there, the counsel was available, but they couldn't benefit from hearing it. So whether through seeing or hearing, guidance found no way to reach them.

translation: Then do those who disbelieve think that they can take My servants instead of Me as allies? Indeed, We have prepared Hell for the disbelievers as a lodging
commentary:
أَفَحَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ عِبَادِى مِن دُونِىٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نُزُلًا

"Then do those who disbelieve think that they can take My servants instead of Me as allies? Indeed, We have prepared Hell for the disbelievers as a lodging."

Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: Do these disbelievers actually think they can take His slaves as protectors and allies instead of Him? This addresses multiple forms of shirk (associating partners with Allah). On one level, it critiques those who worshipped idols—statues they themselves had made, often based on righteous people from their past. On another level, it addresses those who placed their hope in human saviors rather than Allah ﷻ.

The word نُزُلًا is particularly striking. Nuzul refers to the initial hospitality you receive when visiting someone's home—the tea, snacks, and light refreshments served while you wait for the main meal. It's the welcoming treatment that signals more is coming. Allah ﷻ uses this word sarcastically: these guests of Hell will be "welcomed" with Hellfire itself as their initial hospitality. And if this is just the nuzul, imagine what comes after.

This sarcasm connects to the materialism that has been criticized throughout the surah. Those who mock Allah's signs and messengers will themselves be mocked through this ironic "hospitality."

## Ayahs 103-104

قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَـٰلًا

ٱلَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا

"Say, 'Shall We inform you of the greatest losers as to [their] deeds? [They are] those whose effort is lost in worldly life, while they think that they are doing well in work.'"

This passage delivers the final verdict on materialism—the theme that runs throughout Al-Kahf. Allah ﷻ asks: Should We tell you who has the absolute worst deeds? These are people whose efforts (سَعْيُهُمْ) were completely exhausted and wasted in this worldly life.

The word sa'y means effort or striving—it could be material effort or even religious effort. But the critical point is the motivation behind it. There can be religious efforts whose ultimate goal is still worldly. Religion itself can become an industry, a means of expanding control and territory. How many civilizations have used religion to justify political and economic expansion? How many armies have been told they're fighting in the path of Allah ﷻ when the real motives are purely political?

The devastating irony is captured in the phrase: "while they think they are doing well in work." These people are proud of what they've built. They look at their accomplishments—whether empires, companies, universities, or even mosques—and think, "Look what I made. This was me. I did this." They take immense pride in their manufacturing, their construction, their achievements.

This is profoundly relevant because it immediately follows the story of Dhul-Qarnayn (AS), who accomplished extraordinary things—building an impenetrable barrier, ruling vast territories, establishing justice across the earth. Yet when his most powerful accomplishment was complete, he immediately attributed the credit to Allah ﷻ. He didn't say, "Look at what I've built. I'm really good at this." The contrast couldn't be clearer.

translation: Say, 'Shall We inform you of the greatest losers as to [their] deeds?'
commentary:
قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَـٰلًا

*Say, 'Shall We inform you of the greatest losers as to their deeds?'*

As Surah Al-Kahf approaches its conclusion, Allah ﷻ presents one of the most frightening questions in the entire Quran. Throughout this surah, we have encountered different forms of trial: the trial of faith in the story of the People of the Cave, the trial of wealth in the story of the two gardens, the trial of knowledge in the journey of Musa (AS) and Al-Khidr, and the trial of power in the account of Dhul-Qarnayn. Now Allah ﷻ brings all of these lessons together and asks a question that forces every human being to examine himself.

قُلْ - 'Say.' The Messenger ﷺ is commanded to directly address humanity. This question is not limited to the disbelievers. It is not limited to a particular nation or generation. Every person who hears these words should feel as though they are being personally addressed.

هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم - 'Shall We inform you?' The word نُنَبِّئُكُم comes from naba', which refers to important news, information of tremendous significance. This is not ordinary information. When the Quran uses the word naba', it refers to something that carries enormous consequences. It is the kind of news that changes how you understand reality itself.

Then Allah ﷻ says: بِٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَـٰلًا - 'the greatest losers in deeds.' Notice that Allah ﷻ does not simply say losers. He says the greatest losers. The word أَخْسَر is the superlative form. These are not people who suffered a small loss. They experienced the worst kind of loss imaginable.

The Quran frequently speaks about profit and loss. This language is deliberate because every human being understands commerce. If someone invests a small amount and loses it, the loss is disappointing. If someone invests his entire fortune and loses everything, the loss becomes catastrophic. But what if a person spends his entire life working, struggling, sacrificing, building and striving only to discover on the Day of Judgment that everything was worthless? What greater loss could there be?

Notice also that Allah ﷻ specifically mentions deeds. Usually when we think of success or failure, we focus on results. But Allah ﷻ directs our attention toward actions themselves. A person may spend decades performing deeds that he believes are valuable. He may be completely convinced that he is succeeding. Yet Allah ﷻ asks whether we would like to know who the greatest losers truly are.

This question immediately creates suspense. Every listener naturally wonders: who are these people? Are they tyrants? Are they criminals? Are they atheists? Are they idol worshippers? The answer that follows is far more shocking than many people expect.

Throughout the surah, Allah ﷻ has repeatedly exposed the danger of illusion. The owner of the gardens imagined his wealth would never disappear. Musa (AS) initially assumed he understood a situation before learning hidden realities. The people of the cave appeared weak and insignificant while actually being honored by Allah. Again and again the surah teaches that appearances can be deceiving.

Now that lesson reaches its climax. The greatest losers are not necessarily those who think they are failing. The most dangerous situation is when a person believes he is succeeding while actually moving toward destruction.

This is why the question is phrased as it is. Allah ﷻ is inviting humanity to examine itself honestly. Before revealing the answer, He forces us to pause and reflect. Could I be among these people? Could my understanding of success be flawed? Could I be investing my life into something that ultimately carries no value with Allah?

There is also a profound connection between this ayah and the modern world. Never in human history have people been more obsessed with measuring success. We count money, followers, subscribers, degrees, achievements, properties and accomplishments. Entire societies revolve around performance and productivity. Yet Allah ﷻ asks a completely different question. Not who gained the most. Not who became the most famous. Not who accumulated the most wealth. Rather: who truly profited before Allah?

The believer therefore learns to evaluate success differently. He understands that worldly measurements can be deceptive. A person may appear successful in every visible sense while being spiritually bankrupt. Another may appear insignificant in the eyes of society while being among the greatest successes with Allah.

The wording of this ayah prepares us for one of the most sobering descriptions in the Quran. It warns us that sincere effort alone is not enough. Hard work alone is not enough. Good intentions by themselves are not enough. What matters is that one's efforts are aligned with truth and guidance. Otherwise a person may spend an entire lifetime building something that ultimately collapses before Allah ﷻ.

The question therefore remains hanging before the listener: Who are the greatest losers in deeds? The answer comes in the next ayah, and it is one of the most terrifying answers the Quran provides. Every believer should approach it with humility, fear and a sincere desire to ensure that his own deeds are accepted by Allah.

translation: Those whose effort is lost in worldly life while they think that they are doing well in work
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا

*Those whose effort is lost in worldly life while they think that they are doing well in work.*

Allah ﷻ now answers the terrifying question posed in the previous ayah. Who are the greatest losers in deeds? The answer is not what many people would expect. The greatest losers are not necessarily those who sat around doing nothing. They are not people who lacked effort, ambition or determination. In fact, the tragedy described in this ayah is far more severe. These are people who spent tremendous effort and energy throughout their lives, yet all of that effort ultimately became lost.

ٱلَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ - 'Those whose striving became lost.' The word سَعْي is particularly important. It does not simply mean action. It refers to active striving, continuous effort and determined movement toward a goal. When someone rushes between Safa and Marwah during Hajj, that movement is connected to this same concept. Sa'y implies effort, struggle, planning and persistence.

Allah ﷻ does not say that their intentions were lost. He says their striving was lost. Think about how frightening that is. Imagine spending decades building something. Imagine investing your intelligence, your youth, your health and your energy into a project. Then imagine discovering that everything amounted to nothing. This is the image Allah ﷻ presents.

The word ضَلَّ literally means to become lost, to wander away from the correct path or to disappear from sight. A traveler crossing a desert who loses the road has dall. Water disappearing into the earth can be described with the same root. Something that was visible, useful and productive suddenly vanishes. Allah ﷻ is telling us that all of their effort disappeared in exactly this way.

Notice that Allah ﷻ says فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - 'in the life of this world.' This is not merely a statement about the Hereafter. Their loss began in this world itself. They spent their worldly existence chasing goals disconnected from divine guidance. They exhausted themselves pursuing things that ultimately could not save them.

This ayah perfectly connects with the central theme of Surah Al-Kahf. Throughout the surah, Allah ﷻ has repeatedly warned us about becoming deceived by appearances. The owner of the two gardens believed his wealth guaranteed his success. The powerful nations confronted by Dhul-Qarnayn measured success through worldly strength. Materialistic societies define success through visible achievements. Again and again, Allah ﷻ exposes the weakness of these assumptions.

Then comes the most shocking part of the ayah: وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا - 'while they think that they are doing excellent work.'

This is what makes their loss so devastating. If a person realizes he is failing, he still has a chance to change. If a traveler knows he is lost, he can seek directions. If a student knows he is failing, he can study harder. The truly dangerous situation is when someone believes he is succeeding while actually moving toward destruction.

Allah ﷻ does not say they hoped they were doing well. He says they thought with confidence that they were doing excellent work. The word يَحْسَبُونَ implies calculation, assumption and certainty. They had convinced themselves that they were successful.

Even more remarkable is the phrase يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا. Allah ﷻ could have simply said that they thought they were doing good deeds. Instead He uses language that implies craftsmanship and excellence. These people viewed themselves as masters of what they were doing. They believed they were producing something impressive. They saw themselves as builders, reformers, visionaries and achievers.

This ayah therefore attacks one of the greatest spiritual diseases: self-deception. Human beings possess an incredible ability to justify themselves. A person can convince himself that nearly anything is good if it serves his desires. Entire societies can collectively convince themselves that falsehood is progress. History repeatedly demonstrates this phenomenon.

Many of the worst crimes committed by humanity were committed by people who believed they were improving the world. Tyrants often think they are bringing order. Corrupt leaders claim they are serving justice. Oppressive systems present themselves as enlightened and civilized. The danger is not merely wrongdoing. The danger is wrongdoing accompanied by certainty that one is right.

This is why believers are constantly taught to seek guidance from Allah ﷻ. Left to ourselves, we can easily become trapped inside our own assumptions. The owner of the gardens was convinced that his wealth proved Allah's favor. Qarun believed his riches were the result of his own knowledge. Iblis believed his reasoning was superior. In every case, arrogance prevented self-correction.

There is also a profound warning here for religious people. Many scholars explained that this ayah is not limited to obvious disbelievers. Any person who acts without sincerity or without proper guidance risks falling into its warning. A deed requires two conditions for acceptance: sincerity for Allah and conformity to His guidance. Remove either one, and the deed becomes corrupted.

Someone may dedicate his life to religious activity yet seek praise from people. Another may be sincere but invent practices Allah never legislated. Both are in danger because effort alone is not enough. The path must be correct and the intention must be pure.

In our modern age, this warning becomes even more relevant. Society constantly tells people to 'follow your truth,' 'believe in yourself' and 'define success however you choose.' The Quran presents the opposite lesson. Success cannot be defined by personal preference. Success is defined by Allah ﷻ. Guidance comes from revelation, not from human desire.

The believer therefore develops a habit of constant self-examination. He does not merely ask whether he is working hard. He asks whether he is working correctly. He does not merely ask whether people approve of his efforts. He asks whether Allah approves. He does not merely measure results. He measures sincerity.

This ayah is among the most humbling in the Quran because it forces every believer to pause and reflect. Am I merely busy, or am I guided? Am I striving toward Allah, or am I only striving toward my own ambitions? Am I seeking acceptance from Allah, or acceptance from people?

The greatest losers are not those who lacked effort. The greatest losers are those who spent their lives striving in the wrong direction while remaining convinced they were succeeding. For this reason, the believer constantly asks Allah ﷻ for guidance, humility and acceptance, fearing not only failure itself but the far greater tragedy of failing while believing he has succeeded.

translation: Those are the ones who disbelieve in the verses of their Lord and in [their] meeting Him, so their deeds have become worthless; and We will not assign to them on the Day of Resurrection any importance
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَآئِهِۦ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَزْنًا

"Those are the ones who disbelieve in the verses of their Lord and in [their] meeting Him, so their deeds have become worthless, and We will not assign to them on the Day of Resurrection any importance."

Allah ﷻ identifies who these people are: those who disbelieved in the ayat (revelations and miraculous signs) of their Master and disbelieved in meeting Him. This is the root problem. How could anyone take credit for their accomplishments if they truly believed they would stand before Allah ﷻ and be questioned about them? If meeting Allah ﷻ was real to them, they couldn't possibly be so arrogant about their achievements.

The consequence is severe: فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ—their deeds have been nullified, rendered worthless. The word a'mal typically refers to good deeds, but here even those deeds that appeared good on the surface are seized, confiscated, made void.

The ayah concludes: "We will not establish for them on the Day of Judgment any weight." Their deeds will have no value, no substance, no worth whatsoever. They will stand empty-handed before their Creator.

translation: That is their recompense - Hell - for what they denied and [because] they took My signs and My messengers in ridicule
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَرُسُلِى هُزُوًا

"That is their recompense—Hell—for what they disbelieved and [because] they took My signs and My messengers in ridicule."

Their recompense is Hellfire because of their disbelief, and because they took Allah's signs and His messengers as a joke—something to mock and ridicule. Notice the parallel: they made mockery of Allah's revelation and messengers, so Allah ﷻ responds with His own form of "mockery" by calling Hellfire their nuzul, their welcoming hospitality.

This reveals a profound truth about materialism: the most materialistic people inevitably make fun of revelation and messengers. Why? Because messengers and revelation share one fundamental message—they want to direct your life in a way that makes the Hereafter your priority and this world merely a means to achieve success in the akhirah. Materialistic people cannot stand this message, so it becomes a source of jokes and ridicule for them.

## Ayahs 107-108

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا

"Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—they will have the Gardens of Paradise as a lodging, wherein they abide eternally. They will not desire from it any transfer."

After describing the fate of the materialistic disbelievers, Allah ﷻ now describes the believing doers of righteous deeds. There's a beautiful hadith where the Prophet ﷺ praised those who would come in later generations, saying one of them would be equivalent to ten of the Sahaba (RA). The Companions thought he might have misspoken, but he clarified: "No, I meant what I said." Why? Because these later believers would have faith in Allah's Messenger ﷺ without ever having seen him. They would live in the most difficult of times, when the world has been beautified and temptations are everywhere, yet they would still choose allegiance to the Messenger ﷺ and his cause over all the attractions of this world.

These are the people of the highest rank. Their lodging—their nuzul—will be Jannatul Firdaus, the highest level of Paradise. But notice the brilliant wordplay: nuzul means you're anticipating something better to come, right? It's the appetizer before the main course. Yet Allah ﷻ says: خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا—"They will not desire any change from it."

The word حِوَلًا comes from tahweel, which means change or transfer. Even though this is their nuzul, implying more should come, they won't want anything else. They'll be so completely satisfied that they won't desire any change whatsoever. Yet Allah ﷻ will keep giving them more anyway. Every time they receive something new, they'll think, "This is perfect. Why would I want anything different?" But Allah ﷻ will continue to bless them beyond their imagination.

Compare this to our homes in this dunya. We think a house is perfect for maybe a week. Then we start thinking: "If the backyard was just a little bigger, it would be amazing. Maybe we should add a pool. What if we knocked down this wall and extended that room?" We're constantly thinking of changes, improvements, modifications. Not so in Jannah. Complete and perfect contentment, even as Allah ﷻ increases His blessings.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds - they will have the Gardens of Paradise as a lodging
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا

*Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds - they will have the Gardens of Paradise as a lodging.*

After presenting one of the most terrifying descriptions in the Quran, Allah ﷻ now turns to one of its most hopeful promises. The previous verses described the greatest losers, those whose efforts were wasted while they imagined themselves successful. Now Allah ﷻ presents the opposite reality: the true winners.

Throughout Surah Al-Kahf, Allah ﷻ has repeatedly shown us two paths. The young believers of the cave stood opposite the disbelieving society around them. The humble believer stood opposite the arrogant owner of the gardens. Musa (AS) learned humility while others assumed knowledge belonged entirely to them. Dhul-Qarnayn used power in service to Allah while others used power for corruption. Again and again the surah has contrasted truth and falsehood, guidance and misguidance, reality and illusion.

Now the final result of those choices is revealed.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - 'Indeed, those who believed.' The word إِنَّ immediately emphasizes certainty. There is no doubt about this promise. Allah ﷻ does not merely mention good people in a general sense. He begins with faith. This is significant because throughout the surah we have seen that correct belief is the foundation upon which everything else is built. The people of the cave were saved because of their faith. The owner of the gardens was ruined because of the corruption of his faith. Every story in the surah revolves around what a person believes about Allah, this world and the Hereafter.

But faith alone is not mentioned by itself. Allah ﷻ immediately adds: وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - 'and performed righteous deeds.' Once again the Quran joins belief and action together. Genuine faith produces action just as a healthy tree produces fruit. Throughout the surah, we have repeatedly encountered the theme of action. The greatest losers were defined by their deeds. The righteous are likewise defined by their deeds. Islam is not a religion of abstract belief disconnected from life. Faith must manifest itself through worship, character, sacrifice and obedience.

Then Allah ﷻ gives the reward: كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا.

The mention of جَنَّات immediately evokes the image of gardens, but the Quranic concept of Jannah is far richer than what comes to mind when we hear the English word 'garden.' The root ج ن ن carries the meaning of being covered or concealed. A garden is called a jannah because its vegetation is so dense that it conceals the ground beneath it. Paradise is a place whose beauty exceeds what the human mind can fully comprehend.

Allah ﷻ does not merely say they will have gardens. He says جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ - the Gardens of Al-Firdaws. The word Firdaws is among the most majestic names of Paradise found in the Quran. Many scholars described it as the highest and most elevated region of Jannah. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ instructed believers that when they ask Allah for Paradise, they should ask specifically for Al-Firdaws.

Why mention Firdaws here? Because the entire surah has been teaching us not to become impressed by the attractions of this world. The owner of the two gardens possessed extraordinary wealth and beauty. Yet his gardens disappeared. Every beauty in this world eventually fades. Every possession eventually vanishes. Every kingdom eventually falls. Therefore Allah ﷻ concludes by directing our attention toward a garden that never perishes.

Notice the contrast. Earlier in the surah, the wealthy man looked at his worldly garden and believed it would never disappear. Here Allah ﷻ presents a garden that truly will never disappear. The worldly garden was temporary and deceptive. Al-Firdaws is eternal and real.

Then Allah ﷻ describes it as نُزُلًا. This is a fascinating word. In Arabic, nuzul refers to what is prepared for an honored guest upon arrival. Imagine a traveler arriving after a long journey. His host prepares food, accommodation and comfort before he even settles in. This welcoming hospitality is called nuzul.

The remarkable implication is that Allah ﷻ describes Al-Firdaws itself as merely the welcoming reception.

Think about that for a moment.

The greatest reward human beings can imagine is described as the initial hospitality. If the welcome itself is Al-Firdaws, then what comes afterward? What greater blessings remain beyond the first reception?

This understanding led many scholars to point out that the greatest reward of Paradise is not its rivers, palaces or gardens. The greatest reward is the pleasure of Allah ﷻ and the opportunity to behold Him. Everything else serves as preparation for that supreme blessing.

There is another beautiful connection to the beginning of the surah. The young believers abandoned comfort, luxury and security for the sake of Allah. They left homes and wealth behind and sought refuge in a cave. A cave is among the humblest forms of shelter imaginable. Yet because they chose Allah over worldly comfort, Allah ﷻ ultimately offers His believing servants the greatest lodging imaginable.

The contrast is extraordinary. They exchanged a temporary shelter for an eternal residence. They left behind the comforts of a city and received the hospitality of Paradise.

This ayah also teaches us how to define success. Modern society often measures success through possessions, influence and achievement. But Allah ﷻ reduces success to two realities: faith and righteous action. Whoever possesses these has succeeded even if he appears insignificant in the eyes of the world. Whoever lacks these has failed even if he appears powerful and accomplished.

The placement of this ayah after the warning about the greatest losers is deliberate. Allah ﷻ does not want believers to live only in fear. Nor does He want them to live only in hope. The Quran constantly balances warning and glad tidings. Fear protects the believer from complacency, while hope protects him from despair.

Thus, after hearing about people whose deeds became worthless, the believer immediately hears about people whose deeds were accepted. After hearing about devastating loss, he hears about eternal success. After hearing about destruction, he hears about Paradise.

This balance reflects one of the most beautiful characteristics of the Quran itself. It constantly guides the servant back toward Allah through both fear and hope. And here, near the conclusion of Surah Al-Kahf, Allah ﷻ reminds us that all the trials discussed throughout the surah ultimately lead to a single destination for those who remain faithful: the Gardens of Al-Firdaws, prepared as an honored welcome from the Lord of the worlds.

translation: Wherein they abide eternally. They will not desire from it any transfer
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا

*Abiding therein forever. They will not desire from it any transfer.*

This ayah continues the description of the believers' reward and concludes the promise that began in the previous verse. After informing us that the believers will be welcomed into the Gardens of Al-Firdaws, Allah ﷻ now describes one of the most remarkable qualities of Paradise: complete and everlasting satisfaction.

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا - 'Remaining therein forever.' Throughout Surah Al-Kahf, the temporary nature of this worldly life has been repeatedly emphasized. The people of the cave slept for centuries only to awaken and discover how brief worldly existence truly is. The owner of the gardens watched his wealth vanish. Nations rose and fell. Kingdoms appeared and disappeared. Every story in the surah reminds us that worldly life is temporary.

Now Allah ﷻ presents the opposite reality. Paradise is not temporary. It is not a reward that eventually expires. It is not a blessing that fades with time. The believers will remain there forever.

The word خَـٰلِدِينَ comes from khulud, permanence and continuity. Human beings have never experienced true permanence in this world. Everything we know eventually changes. Relationships change. Circumstances change. Wealth changes. Health changes. Even mountains erode over time. The human experience is one of constant transition.

This is one reason worldly happiness can never be complete. Even during moments of joy, a person knows they cannot last forever. Every celebration eventually ends. Every achievement eventually becomes a memory. Every possession eventually deteriorates.

But Allah ﷻ removes this anxiety completely in Paradise. There is no fear of loss because there is no loss. There is no fear of endings because there are no endings. There is no fear of separation because there is no separation.

Then Allah ﷻ says something extraordinary:

لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا

'They will not desire from it any transfer.'

The word يَبْغُونَ means to seek, desire or pursue something. Human beings are naturally restless creatures. We constantly seek change. A person buys a new phone and becomes excited for a few weeks. Soon another model appears. Someone purchases a house and spends years dreaming of a bigger one. A child wants to become a teenager. A teenager wants to become an adult. An adult wishes he were young again.

This restlessness is deeply embedded within worldly life.

Even when people attain something they desperately wanted, satisfaction often fades quickly. Psychologists call this adaptation. Islam described the phenomenon centuries ago. The human being becomes accustomed to blessings and begins searching for something new.

Allah ﷻ tells us that Paradise is fundamentally different.

The believers will never experience boredom.

They will never feel trapped.

They will never desire a different place.

They will never say, 'I've seen everything here.'

They will never want to leave.

This is one of the most remarkable descriptions of Jannah because it addresses a concern many people quietly have. Human beings can imagine eternal punishment as terrifying, but they sometimes struggle to imagine eternal pleasure. They wonder whether endless existence might eventually become repetitive.

Allah ﷻ answers that concern directly. The inhabitants of Paradise themselves will never desire any replacement.

The word حِوَلًا refers to moving from one state to another, transferring from one place to another or exchanging one thing for something else. In this world, people constantly seek hawl. They move cities. They change careers. They renovate homes. They redesign possessions. They pursue novelty because worldly pleasures inevitably become familiar.

But Paradise is different because its blessings are not static.

The delights of Jannah are continuously renewed. Every experience contains fresh joy. Every blessing contains deeper beauty. Every moment contains greater pleasure than the one before it. The excitement never fades because Allah ﷻ continuously increases His servants in enjoyment.

This connects beautifully with an earlier passage in the surah. In ayah 3, Allah ﷻ described the believers as مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا - remaining forever. The commentary there highlighted the concept of anticipation. The people of Paradise are not merely enjoying what they possess; they are constantly anticipating even greater blessings. That anticipation never ends.

Imagine living in the most beautiful place imaginable. Then imagine that every day reveals something more beautiful than the day before. Every gathering is more joyful. Every meeting more fulfilling. Every blessing greater than the last. Such a person would never seek another destination because every moment confirms that he is already in the best possible place.

There is another subtle contrast with the owner of the two gardens earlier in the surah. He believed his worldly garden would remain forever. He imagined permanence where none existed. Allah ﷻ destroyed his illusion. Now Allah ﷻ presents the true eternal garden. Unlike the garden of this world, Al-Firdaws genuinely endures.

Likewise, worldly people spend their lives chasing satisfaction yet rarely attain it. They constantly move from one desire to another. One achievement leads to another ambition. One possession leads to another craving. The cycle never ends. Paradise is the only place where that search finally comes to rest.

The deeper lesson of this ayah is that every longing of the human soul ultimately points toward Allah ﷻ. Our dissatisfaction with worldly things is not accidental. Allah ﷻ designed this world to be incapable of completely satisfying us. Every worldly blessing contains some imperfection. Every pleasure contains some limitation. Every joy contains the possibility of loss.

These imperfections teach us that we were created for something greater.

We were not created for temporary gardens.

We were created for Al-Firdaws.

We were not created for fleeting happiness.

We were created for eternal happiness.

We were not created for a world of endings.

We were created for a world without endings.

Thus this brief ayah provides one of the most comforting promises in the Quran. The believers will enter a home from which they never wish to depart, a blessing that never diminishes, a joy that never fades and a Paradise whose beauty never becomes ordinary. There, for the first time in human existence, every longing will be fulfilled and every search will finally come to an end.

translation: Say, "If the sea were ink for [writing] the words of my Lord, the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted, even if We brought the like of it as a supplement
commentary:
قُل لَّوْ كَانَ ٱلْبَحْرُ مِدَادًا لِّكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّى لَنَفِدَ ٱلْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَن تَنفَدَ كَلِمَـٰتُ رَبِّى وَلَوْ جِئْنَا بِمِثْلِهِۦ مَدَدًا

"Say, 'If the sea were ink for [writing] the words of my Lord, the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted, even if We brought the like of it as a supplement.'"

The surah concludes with praise of the Quran—beautifully parallel to how it began with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ. Allah ﷻ instructs His Prophet ﷺ: Tell them that if all the oceans were turned into ink for writing the words of your Master, the oceans would run out before the words of Allah ﷻ would be exhausted.

The word مِدَادًا is a beautiful choice. The common Arabic word for ink is حِبْرٌ, but midadan comes from مَدَّ, which means to extend. Ink becomes the extension of your hand, the extension of your thoughts, the extension of your understanding—a part of you flows onto the paper. The word midadan also implies a vast, extensive amount of ink—not just an inkpot but an ocean of ink. It's the perfect word for properly communicating what Allah ﷻ intends to express.

But what are the "words of Allah"? This has two dimensions. On one level, it refers to His revelations—the Quran and previous scriptures. On another level, it encompasses every instruction Allah ﷻ gives, every matter He executes. At any given moment, how many things exist in creation? How many instructions is Allah ﷻ giving? How many matters is He managing, controlling, executing? If you tried to document even a fraction of the instructions and management of Allah ﷻ, entire oceans wouldn't suffice as ink. The oceans would dry up قَبْلَ أَن تَنفَدَ كَلِمَـٰتُ رَبِّى—before the words of your Lord would run out.

Even if We brought another ocean like it to continue writing, it still wouldn't be enough. This is the magnificence and infinity of Allah's knowledge, wisdom and control.

translation: Say, "I am only a man like you, to whom has been revealed that your god is one God. So whoever would hope for the meeting with his Lord - let him do righteous work and not associate in the worship of his Lord anyone
commentary:
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَحَدًۢا

"Say, 'I am only a man like you, to whom has been revealed that your god is one God. So whoever would hope for the meeting with his Lord—let him do righteous work and not associate in the worship of his Lord anyone.'"

After highlighting the greatness of the Quran, Allah ﷻ immediately instructs His Messenger ﷺ to emphasize his own humility. The Quran's greatness belongs to Allah ﷻ alone, even though the medium of its delivery is the Prophet ﷺ himself. He wants to make absolutely sure that when people praise the word of Allah ﷻ, they don't confuse that with praising him. "Tell them: I am just a human being like you. I'm flesh and bone. The credit doesn't go to me. The only distinction is that revelation has been given to me, revealing that your God is One God."

Then comes the powerful conclusion: فَمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ رَبِّهِۦ—"Whoever was hopeful of meeting with their Lord." This is where materialism dies. When someone develops within themselves a genuine hope and longing to meet Allah ﷻ, they stop being materialistic. Nothing else holds the same appeal. Someone might desire wealth, luxury, beautiful possessions—but when you cultivate within yourself the desire to meet Allah ﷻ, none of those things will have the same taste. That yearning overshadows everything else.

Such a person should do two things:

First: فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا—"Let him do righteous work." Notice how the surah began by highlighting action (لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا—to test which of you is best in deed) and now ends the same way. Action, action, action. This is particularly significant because the surah opened with an address to Christians, and one of the theological problems in Christianity is the separation of faith from action. The Mosaic Law was considered abolished; moral values remained, but actions weren't considered essential for salvation. "You're already saved because you have Jesus in your heart. It's what's in your heart that matters, not what you do."

This disconnect between faith and action has created a phenomenon where people can engage in terrible crimes yet still consider themselves saved because they attend church and wear a crucifix. Allah ﷻ makes it crystal clear: faith and action are inseparable.

Second: وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَحَدًۢا—"And let him not associate anyone in the worship of his Lord." Don't commit any form of shirk with Allah ﷻ. This connects back to the beginning of the surah where Allah ﷻ refuted those who said He took a son (وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا). The conversation began by addressing the Christian theological problem, and it ends by alluding to it again.

This is the beautiful coherence of Surah Al-Kahf—it opens with the Quran's praise and the refutation of shirk, takes us through four powerful stories about faith, materialism, knowledge, and power, and concludes by returning to the Quran's infinite worth and the call to pure, action-based monotheism.
